input.plan:
# Plan: `vc-x1 show-session` — display a Claude Code transcript

## Context

A project goal is seeing all bot session conversations and, eventually,
the change associated with a particular prompt. First step: a command
that displays one `.claude/<uuid>.jsonl` transcript as a readable
conversation.

Survey findings (this session): the cct crate parses today's files
without error but silently drops newer fields and lags the format;
weavr (crates.io) shows the right tolerant-parse posture. Decision:
own a small tolerant reader — the format is undocumented and churns
continuously, and the long-term goal (prompt ↔ commit linking via
ochids) is vc-x1-specific anyway.

**Agreed decisions (binding):**
- Two-layer parse: `serde_json` used **only** as text → `Value`
  parser (no derive anywhere); hand-written extraction from `Value`
  into our own plain Rust structs, evolved over time. Unknown fields
  ride along in the retained `Value`; missing fields degrade
  gracefully.
- v1 input: positional FILE path only. (Cross-file references —
  sidechain `agent-*.jsonl`, summary `leafUuid`, compaction chains —
  are real; design the typed layer so following them is additive.)
- v1 output: conversation view. Index view, discovery, and linking
  come in later cycles.
- Implementation runs at **medium effort** (per user); this plan was
  produced at xhigh.

## Data survey (grounding)

Across the project's `.claude/*.jsonl` (~65k lines): assistant 29120,
user 19087, then bookkeeping types (file-history-snapshot 4776,
permission-mode 3064, last-prompt 3016, system 2831, progress 2363,
ai-title 2249, attachment 2050, mode 699, queue-operation 131, …).
- assistant entries: one JSONL line **per content block**
  (tool_use / thinking / text); `message.id` identifies the API turn.
- user entries: `message.content` is a plain string (typed prompt,
  with `promptSource`) or an array of `tool_result` blocks.
- `isMeta` marks injected lines; `isSidechain` marks subagent lines;
  both `sessionId` and `session_id` appear.
- A live session's last line may be truncated — must tolerate.

## Module layout

### New: `src/transcript.rs` — parsing + typed layer (command-agnostic)

```rust
pub struct Transcript {
    pub entries: Vec<Entry>,
    pub malformed: Vec<(usize, String)>,   // (1-based line no, error)
}
pub struct Entry {
    pub line_no: usize,
    pub meta: EntryMeta,
    pub kind: EntryKind,
    pub raw: serde_json::Value,            // unknown fields ride along
}
pub struct EntryMeta {                     // all absent-tolerant
    pub uuid: Option<String>,
    pub parent_uuid: Option<String>,
    pub timestamp: Option<String>,         // raw ISO-8601, unparsed
    pub session_id: Option<String>,        // sessionId, then session_id
    pub is_sidechain: bool,
    pub is_meta: bool,
    pub cwd: Option<String>,
}
pub enum EntryKind {
    User { content: Vec<ContentBlock>, prompt_source: Option<String> },
    Assistant { content: Vec<ContentBlock>, message_id: Option<String> },
    System { subtype: Option<String> },
    Other { entry_type: String },          // every other/unknown type
}
pub enum ContentBlock {
    Text { text: String },
    Thinking { thinking: String },
    ToolUse { id: String, name: String, input: serde_json::Value },
    ToolResult { tool_use_id: String, text: String, is_error: bool },
    Other { block_type: String },
}
```

Functions: `parse_file(&Path) -> Result<Transcript, Box<dyn Error>>`
(errors only on I/O), `parse_str(&str) -> Transcript`, private
`parse_line`, and extraction helpers (`get_str`, `get_bool`,
`extract_content` — string content normalizes to one Text block —
`tool_result_text` handling both string and array-of-text shapes).
No panics: missing `type` → `Other`; truncated last line → one
`malformed` record. Tests in sibling `src/transcript/tests.rs`
(the `common/tests.rs` pattern).

### New: `src/show_session.rs` — command + renderer

`validate_todo.rs` shape: `ShowSessionArgs` (positional
`FILE: PathBuf`; flags `--thinking`, `--tools`, `--meta`) →
`ShowSessionParams { file, opts: RenderOptions }` via `From` →
`show_session(ctx, params)`; `SubcommandRunner` impl. Renderer as
pure private fns returning strings (unit-testable), printed via
`info!`: `render(&Transcript, &RenderOptions) -> (Vec<String>,
RenderStats)`, `tool_use_gist`, `short_time` (safe `.get(11..19)`
slice of the ISO string).

### Edits
- `src/main.rs`: `mod transcript;`, `mod show_session;`,
  `Commands::ShowSession(...)` variant + dispatch arm.
- `Cargo.toml`: `serde_json = "1"` (default features; pulls serde as
  a lib dep — accepted, no derive feature). No chrono change.

## Renderer (conversation view)

```
=== user 04:17:09 ===                 ← common::bold header
Let's do todo 1, move notes/todo.md ./TODO.md

=== assistant 04:17:15 ===
I'll start by reading the top of `notes/todo.md` ...
  [tool] Read: notes/todo.md (offset=0, limit=60)
```

  changes — collapses one-line-per-block into visual turns.

- No `unwrap`/`expect`; project lint conventions apply

## Tests

pub enum ContentBlock {
    Text { text: String },
    Thinking { thinking: String },
    ToolUse { id: String, name: String, input: serde_json::Value },
    ToolResult { tool_use_id: String, text: String, is_error: bool },
    Other { block_type: String },
}
```

Functions: `parse_file(&Path) -> Result<Transcript, Box<dyn Error>>`
(errors only on I/O), `parse_str(&str) -> Transcript`, private
`parse_line`, and extraction helpers (`get_str`, `get_bool`,
`extract_content` — string content normalizes to one Text block —
`tool_result_text` handling both string and array-of-text shapes).
No panics: missing `type` → `Other`; truncated last line → one
`malformed` record. Tests in sibling `src/transcript/tests.rs`
(the `common/tests.rs` pattern).

### New: `src/show_session.rs` — command + renderer

`validate_todo.rs` shape: `ShowSessionArgs` (positional
`FILE: PathBuf`; flags `--thinking`, `--tools`, `--meta`) →
`ShowSessionParams { file, opts: RenderOptions }` via `From` →
`show_session(ctx, params)`; `SubcommandRunner` impl. Renderer as
pure private fns returning strings (unit-testable), printed via
`info!`: `render(&Transcript, &RenderOptions) -> (Vec<String>,
RenderStats)`, `tool_use_gist`, `short_time` (safe `.get(11..19)`
slice of the ISO string).

### Edits
- `src/main.rs`: `mod transcript;`, `mod show_session;`,
  `Commands::ShowSession(...)` variant + dispatch arm.
- `Cargo.toml`: `serde_json = "1"` (default features; pulls serde as
  a lib dep — accepted, no derive feature). No chrono change.

## Renderer (conversation view)

```
=== user 04:17:09 ===                 ← common::bold header
Let's do todo 1, move notes/todo.md ./TODO.md

=== assistant 04:17:15 ===
I'll start by reading the top of `notes/todo.md` ...
  [tool] Read: notes/todo.md (offset=0, limit=60)
```

- Header only when rendered role changes or assistant `message.id`
  changes — collapses one-line-per-block into visual turns.
- Default hides: thinking, tool results, meta/system/sidechain lines,
  all `Other` bookkeeping types. `--thinking` / `--tools` / `--meta`
  reveal (sidechain under `--meta` for v1).
- `tool_use_gist`: Bash → first line of `command`; Read/Write/Edit →
  `file_path`; fallback → compact `key=value` pairs, ~100 chars max.
- Tool results under `--tools`: indented, capped ~10 lines,
  `[result:error]` when `is_error`.
- No wrapping in v1; only one-liners truncate.
- Always end with a summary line: turns shown; hidden thinking /
  tool results / meta counts; skipped bookkeeping; malformed lines
  (omit zero clauses).

## Error handling

- Malformed line → `warn!` to stderr, recorded, continue; exit 0.
- Unknown types → skipped silently, counted in the summary.
- Only hard error: unreadable file (`cannot read {path}: {e}`).
- No `unwrap`/`expect`; project lint conventions apply
  (`// OK:` if one becomes unavoidable — alert in conversation).

## Tests

- `src/transcript/tests.rs` (inline JSON fixtures): typed prompt,
  each assistant block type, tool_result string + array shapes,
  `is_error`, `isMeta`/`isSidechain`, unknown type, missing `type`,
  blank line, truncated last line (count + line_no), `raw` retains
  unextracted fields.
- `show_session.rs` in-module tests: `tool_use_gist` (Bash multi-line,
  Read, unknown tool truncation), `short_time` edge cases, `render`
  on a synthetic Transcript (header collapsing, hide/reveal, stats).
- `tests/cli_show_session.rs` with `CliFixture`: ~10-line fixture
  covering the entry mix + a truncated final line; assert default
  output (prompt + assistant text + `[tool]`, no thinking/result
  bodies, one `warn:`), the three reveal flags, and `run_err` on a
  missing file.

## Cycle ladder (0.70.0 — confirm at Preparation)

Titles share the greppable stem `show-session`:

- `0.70.0-0` `chore: open show-session cycle` — version bump;
  In Progress block in TODO.md (problem + this ladder); open chores
  section in `notes/chores/chores-13.md` (or new -14 if 13 is full).
- `0.70.0-1` `feat: transcript parse + typed layer for show-session`
  — serde_json dep; `src/transcript.rs` + tests; `mod transcript;`.
  Keep clippy clean by having tests exercise the public surface
  (avoid blanket `#[allow(dead_code)]`).
- `0.70.0-2` `feat: show-session command + conversation renderer` —
  `src/show_session.rs`, main.rs wiring, integration tests.
- `0.70.0` close-out — bookkeeping per cycle protocol (Done entry,
  chores fill, notes/README.md update for the new subcommand);
  mandatory full cargo cycle; push per Pushing policy (user approval).

Per-commit flow throughout: mark `(current)`, work, flip `(done)`,
cargo fmt / clippy -D warnings / test / install, **stop for user work
review before describing**, description review, commit. Known risks:
header-collapsing may split oddly around sidechain lines (revisit in
the sidechain cycle); empty `thinking` blocks render nothing even
under `--thinking`.

## Verification

- Full cargo cycle green (fmt, clippy --all-targets -D warnings,
  test, install --path . --locked).
- End-to-end: `vc-x1 show-session .claude/4f5d498c-....jsonl` on the
  real transcript — readable conversation, sane summary line; again
  with `--thinking --tools --meta`; on the *current* live session
  file — truncated-line warning but exit 0.

file.content:
- The AGENTS.md "File reads" section (and cycle-protocol.md's
  two mentions) are part of the shared byte-identical set, so
  this is a three-project change (vc-x1, vc-template-x1,
  iiac-perf) applied identically; vc-x1 went first, breaking
  byte-identity until the other two apply the same change.
- Historical files (chores-NN.md, done.md, dated
  manifests/audits) keep their `notes/todo.md` mentions.
- The no-arg `validate-todo` / `fix-todo` default
  (`TODO_FILE`) had to follow the move — a behavior change
  the original Todo entry's "code behavior is unaffected"
  parenthetical missed (it held only for doc strings).
- Surfaced in review: the validate/fix wrapper layer has no
  tests (the analyze cores do) — noted as a bullet on the
  validate-numbering Todo.

- 0.69.2-2 refactor: TODO.md as validate/fix-todo default

**feat: show-session transcript viewer**

Display one Claude Code session transcript
(`.claude/<uuid>.jsonl`) as a readable conversation — first step
toward seeing all bot sessions and linking prompts to the
changes they produced. Two-layer parse: serde_json as text →
`Value` only (no derive); hand-written extraction into our own
structs that evolve as we learn the format. v1 is file-path in,
conversation view out; index view, session discovery, and
cross-file references (sidechain `agent-*.jsonl`, compaction
chains) come in later cycles. Full design in the approved plan
(bot session of 2026-07-17).

- 0.70.0-1 feat: transcript parse + typed layer for show-session
- 0.70.0-2 feat: show-session command + conversation renderer
- 0.70.0 close-out and validation

the references as part of the "As-built ladder" so it's easy to know the correlation.
For this round lets keep "Commits: .." but let's prepend a reference to each
"As-built ladder" so something like:
```
### As-built ladder

- [[33]] 0.69.2-0 docs: open TODO.md move cycle
- [[34]] 0.69.2-1 docs: move notes/todo.md to TODO.md
- [[35]] 0.69.2-2 refactor: TODO.md as validate/fix-todo default
- [[36]] 0.69.2 docs: move todo.md to root TODO.md (close-out)
```

Thoughts?

file.content:
## docs: shared protocol sync + jj refactor plan

Commits: [[31]],[[32]]

Two docs threads landed together (first title under the new
no-version-suffix convention — see versioning.md, adopted this
commit).

- Shared protocol sync: adopt the vc-template-x1 shared set —
  AGENTS.md, cycle-protocol.md, versioning.md (new),
  jj-tips.md (new, supersedes jj-revsets.md) — with vc-x1's
  0.69.0 corrections applied and ratified template-side
  (inline squash-push flow restored, current stage names,
  preflight described as state-checks-only, roadmap material
  de-projected from the wrapper section). The per-file
  what/why is
  [notes-sync-20260716.md](../notes-sync-20260716.md); the
  goal is a byte-identical shared set across iiac-perf,
  vc-x1, vc-template-x1.
- jj refactor program: the `bookmark-set` index-lock race
  investigation (bugs.md entry 3) grew into a staged program
  — typed jj facade → jj-lib in-process, ending subprocess
  spawning — absorbing eight existing Todos; plan and design
  in [refactor-20260716.md](../refactor-20260716.md) (the
  first dated plan file, one `##` section per stage for
  stable anchors). todo.md restructured around it; the
  TODO.md-move entry added as Todo #1.
- [[32]] (`docs: converge shared protocol doc set`) is the
  follow-up review round: the four fixes agreed across the
  three sessions, converging the shared set byte-identical
  (agreed sha256s recorded in its commit body).

## docs: move todo.md to root TODO.md

Commits: [[33]],[[34]],[[35]]

The todo list is the project's live state and the routine
acquaint read; root-level uppercase puts it in the
conventional root-file family (README, LICENSE, AGENTS.md,
ARCHITECTURE.md) — the same "easy for everyone to find"

content.text:
Backfill applied, all within the pending `0.70.0-0` commit:

- `Commits:` on the 0.69.2 section now cites `[[33]],[[34]],[[35]],[[36]]`
- The As-built ladder carries per-rung refs exactly as you sketched
- New definition `[36]` → `48886d3e38b6` (full 40-hex in the title slot)

Two follow-on points for your call, neither blocking this commit:

1. **The convention's home**: cycle-protocol.md's Close-out section ("Add an `### As-built ladder`…") is in the byte-identical shared doc set, so I'd log "As-built ladder rungs carry `[[N]]` commit refs" as a Todo/backlog entry for a coordinated three-project doc sync rather than edit it mid-cycle. Want me to add that entry now (it would ride in this prep commit)?
2. **Older ladders** (0.69.0 and earlier in chores-13, plus prior files) stay ref-less per the "convention applies going forward" rule; a retrofit can happen opportunistically if it ever earns its keep.

Otherwise the Preparation commit is ready for your review as amended — same title proposal: `chore: open show-session cycle`.

content.content:
318	## Pushing
319	
320	### Policy
321	
322	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
323	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
324	the cycle's result must be published.
325	
326	**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
327	cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
328	happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
329	published and explicitly approved that specific push.
330	Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
331	the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.
332	
333	**Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
334	the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
335	commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
336	hard stop after push/squash-push — are the *interactive
337	default*.
338	They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
339	bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
340	and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
341	through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
342	continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:
343	
344	- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
345	  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
346	  completion. "Commit and push" (or "then push") names the
347	  destination, not a waiver: it authorizes the push *after*
348	  the normal work review and description review, not skipping
349	  them. Only wording that explicitly waives the stops ("don't
350	  check in", "no need to review", "carry it through
351	  unattended") waives them.
352	- **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
353	  to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
354	- **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
355	  ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
356	  genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
357	  history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
358	  permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
359	  bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
360	  reaches this.
361	- **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
362	  (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
363	- **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
364	  per-push approval.
365	
366	### Shape at close-out push
367	
368	At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
369	shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
370	the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
371	the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
372	needs approval), so choose deliberately.
373	
374	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
375	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
376	  focused on one or two files.
377	- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
378	  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
379	  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
380	  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
381	  for the full setup sequence.
382	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
383	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
384	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
385	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
386	
387	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
388	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
389	
390	### Merge non-ff recipe
391	
392	Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
393	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
394	close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
395	(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
396	Work commit:
397	
398	1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
399	   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
400	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
401	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
402	   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.
403	
404	   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
405	   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
406	2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
407	   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
408	   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
409	   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
410	3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.
411	
412	**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
413	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
414	immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
415	push force-updates `main`.
416	The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
417	the close-out push.
418	
419	### vc-x1 push wrapper
420	
421	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
422	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags. `<bookmark>` names a
423	work-repo bookmark only; the bot repo is always pinned to
424	`main` (see [.claude cadence](#claude-cadence)).
425	
426	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
427	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
428	shapes need manual jj steps. Planned improvements are
429	project state, tracked in the project's `TODO.md` —
430	this protocol describes only the stable mechanism.
431	
432	### .claude cadence
433	
434	**Cadence**: one push = one bot-repo commit, paired
435	with every work-repo commit in that push.
436	
437	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
438	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
439	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the squash-push fold, so
440	work-repo `ochid:` trailers resolve.
441	
442	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
443	on `main`, regardless of the work-repo bookmark. **Do
444	not create or maintain bot-repo bookmarks that mirror
445	work-repo branches** — risks the bot steering session
446	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
447	
448	Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
449	session information created, which we don't worry about.
450	The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
451	remain empty.
452	
453	### Bot communication at the reviews
454	
455	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
456	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
457	
458	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
459	  "Work complete. Please review."
460	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
461	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
462	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
463	  --body ...` invocation by default.
464	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
465	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
466	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
467	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

file.content:
## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
published and explicitly approved that specific push.
Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.

**Default is interactive; an explicit scoped delegation waives
the gates.** The gates above — per-push approval, the
commit-description review (show title+body and stop), and the
hard stop after push/squash-push — are the *interactive
default*.
They yield when the user **explicitly** delegates a complete,
bounded task and authorizes carrying it through ("do all of X
and push each step, don't check in"). The bot then proceeds
through that task's commits and pushes without stopping, and
continues past each push to the next step. Conditions:

- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
  completion. "Commit and push" (or "then push") names the
  destination, not a waiver: it authorizes the push *after*
  the normal work review and description review, not skipping
  them. Only wording that explicitly waives the stops ("don't
  check in", "no need to review", "carry it through
  unattended") waives them.
- **Bounded goal** — covers the named task only; does not carry
  to the next task or a vaguer follow-on.
- **Destructive ops still pause** — delegation covers the task's
  ordinary commits and pushes; it does *not* pre-authorize a
  genuinely irreversible action (force-push over published
  history, history rewrite, deleting a remote branch). Those can
  permanently destroy work and aren't a normal cycle step, so the
  bot flags one before acting. An ordinary delegated cycle never
  reaches this.
- **Still transparent** — report each commit/push as it lands
  (title + outcome) so the user can catch up.
- **When in doubt, ask** — ambiguous authorization falls back to
  per-push approval.

### Shape at close-out push

At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
needs approval), so choose deliberately.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.

   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags. `<bookmark>` names a
work-repo bookmark only; the bot repo is always pinned to
`main` (see [.claude cadence](#claude-cadence)).

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Planned improvements are
project state, tracked in the project's `TODO.md` —
this protocol describes only the stable mechanism.

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one bot-repo commit, paired
with every work-repo commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the squash-push fold, so
work-repo `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the work-repo bookmark. **Do
not create or maintain bot-repo bookmarks that mirror
work-repo branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
session information created, which we don't worry about.
The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
remain empty.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

Good check. Looking at it:

- **v1 `show-session` touches no version-control code at all** — the ladder's two work commits are pure file-read + JSONL parse + render. The input is an explicit file path; nothing shells out to jj, nothing reads repo state. So no `run("jj", …)` sites get added, and Todo #1's cleanup surface doesn't grow.

By the time we get there, the right route is jj-lib in-process (already a dependency, `jj-lib = "0.41"`) via whatever typed facade the Todo #1 program has built by then — not new subprocess spawns.

Let me record that constraint in the In Progress block so it's durable rather than conversational:

input.command: vc-x1 push --help
input.description: Show vc-x1 push flags

content.content:
vc-x1 0.69.2

Commit both repos, push the work repo's BOOKMARK, and
squash-push the bot repo's `main` — one resumable command.

Collapses the manual commit-push-publish ceremony into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (tracking / bot-published / sync checks —
no build steps; run project checks yourself before pushing)
→ review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-work → commit-bot (skipped if clean) → bookmark-set
(work repo → <bookmark>, bot repo → main) → push-work →
squash-push-bot. Failures in commit-work / commit-bot /
bookmark-set roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-work.
After push-work succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in the work repo (positional form of
          `--bookmark`). The bot repo always advances `main`.
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in the work repo (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Verify bookmark tracking, the bot-published
            invariant, and sync state
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-work:     Commit the work repo
          - commit-bot:      Commit the `.claude` bot repo (skipped if empty)
          - bookmark-set:    Advance each repo's bookmark to its `@-`: work →
            `<bookmark>`, bot → `main`
          - push-work:       `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - squash-push-bot: In-process squash of `.claude`'s trailing session
            writes + push `main`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

      --no-squash-push
          Stop before `squash-push-bot` so it can be run manually

      --dry-run
          Print the exact commands for every stage without side effects

      --title <STR>
          Commit title (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

      --body <STR>
          Commit body (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

  -y, --yes
          Auto-approve interactive prompts (review gate, message-edit
          confirmation). Required when `--title` / `--body` aren't both supplied
          in non-interactive contexts

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

          Bookmark to advance in the work repo (positional form of
          `--bookmark`). The bot repo always advances `main`.
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in the work repo (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Verify bookmark tracking, the bot-published
            invariant, and sync state
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-work:     Commit the work repo
          - commit-bot:      Commit the `.claude` bot repo (skipped if empty)
          - bookmark-set:    Advance each repo's bookmark to its `@-`: work →
            `<bookmark>`, bot → `main`
          - push-work:       `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - squash-push-bot: In-process squash of `.claude`'s trailing session
            writes + push `main`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

      --no-squash-push
          Stop before `squash-push-bot` so it can be run manually

      --dry-run
          Print the exact commands for every stage without side effects

      --title <STR>
          Commit title (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

      --body <STR>
          Commit body (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

  -y, --yes
          Auto-approve interactive prompts (review gate, message-edit
          confirmation). Required when `--title` / `--body` aren't both supplied
          in non-interactive contexts

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

attachment.snippet:
1	//! Claude Code session transcript (`.jsonl`) parsing.
2	//!
3	//! Two-layer design so schema churn in the (undocumented,
4	//! evolving) transcript format never breaks us:
5	//!
6	//! - serde_json is used only as a JSON-text → `Value` parser; the
7	//!   full parsed line is retained in `Entry::raw`, so unknown
8	//!   fields ride along.
9	//! - Hand-written extraction builds the typed layer (`EntryMeta`,
10	//!   `EntryKind`, `ContentBlock`) — every field degrades to
11	//!   `None`/`false`/empty when absent, and unrecognized entry or
12	//!   block types land in `Other` variants instead of erroring.
13	
14	use serde_json::Value;
15	use std::path::Path;
16	
17	/// A parsed session transcript: every parseable line, in file order.
18	pub struct Transcript {
19	    /// All entries, including ones a conversation view hides.
20	    pub entries: Vec<Entry>,
21	    /// Malformed lines: (1-based line number, parse error text).
22	    /// A live session's truncated final line lands here.
23	    pub malformed: Vec<(usize, String)>,
24	}
25	
26	/// One parsed JSONL line.
27	pub struct Entry {
28	    /// 1-based line number in the source file.
29	    pub line_no: usize,
30	    /// Common metadata shared across entry types.
31	    pub meta: EntryMeta,
32	    /// Classified payload.
33	    pub kind: EntryKind,
34	    /// The full original parsed line; unknown fields ride along.
35	    pub raw: Value,
36	}
37	
38	/// Common top-level fields; each degrades gracefully when absent.
39	pub struct EntryMeta {
40	    /// Unique id of this entry.
41	    pub uuid: Option<String>,
42	    /// Id of the parent entry (conversation threading).
43	    pub parent_uuid: Option<String>,
44	    /// Raw ISO-8601 timestamp string, unparsed.
45	    pub timestamp: Option<String>,
46	    /// Session id (`sessionId`, falling back to `session_id`).
47	    pub session_id: Option<String>,
48	    /// True on subagent (sidechain) lines.
49	    pub is_sidechain: bool,
50	    /// True on injected/meta lines (not typed by the user).
51	    pub is_meta: bool,
52	    /// Working directory at the time of the entry.
53	    pub cwd: Option<String>,
54	}
55	
56	/// Classified payload by top-level `type`.
57	pub enum EntryKind {
58	    /// `type=user`: a typed prompt (string content) or tool
59	    /// results (array content) — both normalized to blocks.
60	    User {
61	        /// Normalized content blocks.
62	        content: Vec<ContentBlock>,
63	        /// `promptSource` — "typed" marks a real human prompt.
64	        prompt_source: Option<String>,
65	    },
66	    /// `type=assistant`: in practice one JSONL line per content
67	    /// block, modeled as a `Vec` to stay shape-agnostic.
68	    Assistant {
69	        /// Normalized content blocks.
70	        content: Vec<ContentBlock>,
71	        /// `message.id` — groups the blocks of one API turn.
72	        message_id: Option<String>,
73	    },
74	    /// `type=system` bookkeeping (e.g. subtype `turn_duration`).
75	    System {
76	        /// The `subtype` field, when present.
77	        subtype: Option<String>,
78	    },
79	    /// Every other `type` (mode, permission-mode, progress,
80	    /// file-history-*, attachment, unknown/future types, …).
81	    Other {
82	        /// The original `type` value ("<none>" when absent).
83	        entry_type: String,
84	    },
85	}
86	
87	/// One block of `message.content`.
88	pub enum ContentBlock {
89	    /// Visible text.
90	    Text {
91	        /// The text body.
92	        text: String,
93	    },
94	    /// Extended-thinking text.
95	    Thinking {
96	        /// The thinking body.
97	        thinking: String,
98	    },
99	    /// A tool invocation.
100	    ToolUse {
101	        /// Tool-use id (pairs with a later `ToolResult`).
102	        id: String,
103	        /// Tool name.
104	        name: String,
105	        /// Tool input, kept as raw JSON.
106	        input: Value,
107	    },
108	    /// A tool's result, sent back on a user line.
109	    ToolResult {
110	        /// The `tool_use_id` this result answers.
111	        tool_use_id: String,
112	        /// Result text (string form, or array-of-text joined).
113	        text: String,
114	        /// True when the tool errored.
115	        is_error: bool,
116	    },
117	    /// Unrecognized block type — counted, never rendered.
118	    Other {
119	        /// The original block `type` value.
120	        block_type: String,
121	    },
122	}
123	
124	/// Parse a whole transcript file.
125	///
126	/// - Errors only on I/O (unreadable file).
127	/// - Malformed lines are recorded in `Transcript::malformed`.
128	pub fn parse_file(path: &Path) -> Result<Transcript, Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
129	    let text = std::fs::read_to_string(path)
130	        .map_err(|e| format!("cannot read {}: {e}", path.display()))?;
131	    Ok(parse_str(&text))
132	}
133	
134	/// Parse transcript text (unit-testable without files).
135	///
136	/// - Blank lines are skipped.
137	/// - Each remaining line becomes an `Entry` or a `malformed`
138	///   record; parsing never fails as a whole.
139	pub fn parse_str(text: &str) -> Transcript {
140	    let mut entries = Vec::new();
141	    let mut malformed = Vec::new();
142	    for (i, line) in text.lines().enumerate() {
143	        let line_no = i + 1;
144	        if line.trim().is_empty() {
145	            continue;
146	        }
147	        match parse_line(line_no, line) {
148	            Ok(entry) => entries.push(entry),
149	            Err(err) => malformed.push((line_no, err)),
150	        }
151	    }
152	    Transcript { entries, malformed }
153	}
154	
155	/// Parse one JSONL line into an `Entry`.
156	///
157	/// - Err on invalid JSON or a non-object top level.
158	/// - Never panics; missing fields degrade per the type docs.
159	fn parse_line(line_no: usize, line: &str) -> Result<Entry, String> {
160	    let raw: Value = serde_json::from_str(line).map_err(|e| e.to_string())?;
161	    if !raw.is_object() {
162	        return Err("top level is not a JSON object".to_string());
163	    }
164	    let meta = extract_meta(&raw);
165	    let kind = extract_kind(&raw);
166	    Ok(Entry {
167	        line_no,
168	        meta,
169	        kind,
170	        raw,
171	    })
172	}
173	
174	/// Extract the common top-level fields.
175	fn extract_meta(v: &Value) -> EntryMeta {
176	    EntryMeta {
177	        uuid: get_str(v, "uuid"),
178	        parent_uuid: get_str(v, "parentUuid"),
179	        timestamp: get_str(v, "timestamp"),
180	        session_id: get_str(v, "sessionId").or_else(|| get_str(v, "session_id")),
181	        is_sidechain: get_bool(v, "isSidechain"),
182	        is_meta: get_bool(v, "isMeta"),
183	        cwd: get_str(v, "cwd"),
184	    }
185	}
186	
187	/// Classify the line by its top-level `type` field.
188	fn extract_kind(v: &Value) -> EntryKind {
189	    let entry_type = get_str(v, "type").unwrap_or_else(|| "<none>".to_string());
190	    match entry_type.as_str() {
191	        "user" => EntryKind::User {
192	            content: extract_content(&v["message"]),
193	            prompt_source: get_str(v, "promptSource"),
194	        },
195	        "assistant" => EntryKind::Assistant {
196	            content: extract_content(&v["message"]),
197	            message_id: get_str(&v["message"], "id"),
198	        },
199	        "system" => EntryKind::System {
200	            subtype: get_str(v, "subtype"),
201	        },
202	        _ => EntryKind::Other { entry_type },
203	    }
204	}
205	
206	/// Normalize `message.content` to blocks.
207	///
208	/// - String content → a single `Text` block (typed prompts).
209	/// - Array content → one block per element by its `type`.
210	/// - Anything else → no blocks.
211	fn extract_content(message: &Value) -> Vec<ContentBlock> {
212	    match &message["content"] {
213	        Value::String(s) => vec![ContentBlock::Text { text: s.clone() }],
214	        Value::Array(items) => items.iter().map(extract_block).collect(),
215	        _ => Vec::new(),
216	    }
217	}
218	
219	/// Extract one content block by its `type` field.
220	fn extract_block(b: &Value) -> ContentBlock {
221	    let block_type = get_str(b, "type").unwrap_or_else(|| "<none>".to_string());

`0.70.0-1` work complete. Please review.

What landed:
- `src/transcript.rs` — the two-layer parser: `parse_file`/`parse_str` → `Transcript { entries, malformed }`; each `Entry` keeps `line_no`, extracted `EntryMeta` (uuid/parent/timestamp/session/flags), classified `EntryKind` (User/Assistant/System/Other), and the full `raw` Value so unknown fields ride along. Content normalizes to `ContentBlock` (Text/Thinking/ToolUse/ToolResult/Other); tool_result handles both string and array-of-text shapes.
- `src/transcript/tests.rs` — 11 unit tests: all block kinds, both tool_result shapes, meta/sidechain flags, unknown/missing types, truncated last line, non-object line, `raw` retention, sessionId fallback, missing-file error.
- `src/main.rs` — `mod transcript;` with a temporary `#[allow(dead_code)]` (commented: consumer lands in `-2`, where the allow is removed).
- `Cargo.toml`/`Cargo.lock` — version `0.70.0-1`; `serde_json = "1"` added.

Two convention alerts:
- **`unwrap_or*` sites** (the non-panicking kind, per the `// OK:` convention): `get_bool` uses `.unwrap_or(false)`, and block extraction uses `.unwrap_or_default()` for `text`/`thinking`/`id`/`name`/`tool_use_id` — all marked `// OK: obvious` (absent field → empty value is the tolerant-parse contract). Missing `type` maps to `"<none>"` via `unwrap_or_else`.
- The `#[allow(dead_code)]` is blanket-on-the-module but one line, commented, and removed next commit.

Validation: `cargo fmt` applied, `clippy --all-targets -D warnings` clean, full test suite green (416 unit + integration suites).

message.content:
Is it possible to move transcript.rs into src/transcript/lib.rs and is that a
common organization, note use a lesser model if the task at hand doesn't benefit
from using fable 5

content.text:
Short answer: `src/transcript/lib.rs` isn't a thing Rust's module system supports — `lib.rs` is specifically the *crate root* filename of a library crate, not a module file. For a module named `transcript` inside this binary crate, the loader looks for exactly two paths: `src/transcript.rs` or `src/transcript/mod.rs`. A `src/transcript/lib.rs` would simply not be found.

So the real choices are:

- **`src/transcript.rs` + `src/transcript/tests.rs`** (what we have) — the Rust 2018+ idiom: the module's own code in `transcript.rs`, submodules in the `transcript/` directory. This is also the repo's established pattern (`common.rs` + `common/tests.rs`, `push.rs` + `push/tests.rs`), so we're consistent.
- **`src/transcript/mod.rs`** — the pre-2018 style, still supported; everything lives under one directory. Some people prefer it for that self-containment, but mixing `mod.rs` style into a codebase that uses the file+directory style is generally discouraged, and rustc docs recommend the newer style for new code.
- **A real `lib.rs`** — i.e., promote transcript parsing to its own library crate (workspace member, `crates/transcript/src/lib.rs`). That *is* common when code is shared across binaries or published, and could make sense someday if the transcript reader grows into something other tools use — but it's overkill for one consumer inside a single-crate project.

So: current layout is already the conventional one; nothing to move.

The plain version: `lib.rs` is a reserved name for "this whole folder is a standalone library," not a way to organize one module. What we have now is the standard modern layout, and it matches how the rest of this repo is organized.

Noted on the model point — I'll hand mechanical or simple subtasks (bulk searches, boilerplate sweeps) to a lesser-model subagent and reserve Fable for design and tricky work. If you want that as a durable project rule, I can add a line to AGENTS.md; say the word. Otherwise, ready to proceed to `0.70.0-2` (command + renderer) when you are.

input.old_string: ## Plain synopsis after technical explanations
input.new_string:
## Model delegation

When spawning subagents, match the model to the task: use a
lesser model (e.g. Haiku or Sonnet) for mechanical or simple
subtasks — bulk searches, boilerplate sweeps, formulaic edits —
and reserve the top model for design and tricky work.

**Why:** top-model tokens are the scarce resource; mechanical
subtasks don't benefit from them, and delegating them keeps
cost and latency down at no quality loss.

/// Check todo-file entry numbering and indent
    #[command(
        long_about = "Check a todo file's `## Todo` and `## Bugs` entry numbering.\n\n\
        Verifies each section is numbered 1..N in document order and\n\
        that continuation-line indent matches the number-prefix width.\n\
        Read-only; exits non-zero if any entry needs fixing — use\n\
        `fix-todo` to rewrite."
    )]
    ValidateTodo(validate_todo::ValidateTodoArgs),

    /// Renumber todo-file entries (dry-run by default)
    #[command(
        long_about = "Renumber a todo file's `## Todo` and `## Bugs` sections.\n\n\
        Renumbers each section 1..N in document order and normalizes\n\
        continuation-line indent to the number-prefix width. Dry-run\n\
        by default — prints each changed entry's corrected line; pass\n\
        --no-dry-run to write the file in place."
    )]
    FixTodo(fix_todo::FixTodoArgs),

    /// Clone a dual-repo project
    Clone(clone::CloneArgs),

    /// Create a new dual-repo project
    Init(init::InitArgs),

    /// Create Claude Code project symlink
    Symlink(symlink::SymlinkArgs),

# Bugs

Known defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for.
Each entry describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of
the failure. Entries are numbered (`1.` `2.` …) the same way
as `## Todo` in `TODO.md`; run
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/bugs.md` to renumber after
insert / delete / reorder.

## Bugs

1. **`init --repo local` bare remotes keep HEAD at
   `refs/heads/master`.** The only branch pushed is `main`,
   so a later `jj git clone` of that bare repo has no default
   branch to auto-track and `vc-x1 clone` fails its
   `verify_tracking` check ("bookmark 'main' has non-tracking
   remote 'main@origin'"). Found building `tests/cli_sync.rs`
   (worked around there with `git symbolic-ref HEAD
   refs/heads/main`).
   - **Fix direction:** init's local bare provisioning sets
     HEAD to `refs/heads/main` at creation.

2. **`clone` session-remote derivation mismatches init's
   local naming; relative TARGET breaks the session clone.**
   Two related defects, both found building
   `tests/cli_sync.rs`:
   - `derive_session_url` maps `<x>.git` →
     `<x>.claude.git`, but `init --repo local` names the
     session remote `remote-claude.git` — so a dual clone of
     a locally-init'd project's `remote-code.git` looks for
     `remote-code.claude.git` and fails.
   - A relative local-path TARGET fails on the session side
     regardless: `clone_dual` runs the session `jj git
     clone` with the just-cloned code repo as cwd, so the
     relative source no longer resolves. Workaround: pass an
     absolute TARGET.

3. **`push` `bookmark-set` races the git index lock.**
   `jj bookmark set` on the colocated work repo failed twice
   (the 0.69.0-3 and 0.69.0-4 pushes, same stage) with
   "Failed to reset Git HEAD state … could not acquire lock
   for `.git/index` … after 1 attempt(s)"; the lockfile was
   already gone on inspection seconds later.
   - **Cost:** push aborts mid-flow (rollback restored both
     repos cleanly both times); recovery is a `--restart`
     rerun, which succeeded both times.
   - The bot thinks a git-aware watcher (shell prompt,
     editor) briefly re-reads the repo after the commit
     stages touch `.git`, holding the index lock exactly
     when `bookmark-set` resets git HEAD; jj gives up after
     a single attempt.
   - **Fix direction** (two options, possibly combined):
     - retry with short backoff around the bookmark-set
       stage (or all jj invocations that reset git HEAD
       state)
     - use jj-lib in-process instead of spawning `jj`
       commands (suggested 2026-07-15) — the lock
       contention is external, so this alone doesn't
       remove the race, but the retry loop becomes ours
       (catch the lock error, back off, retry) with real
       error types instead of stderr parsing; this is the
       refactor program's
       [jj-lib migration stage](refactor-20260716.md#stage-jj-lib-migration)

# References

4. **stdout output panics on a closed pipe (EPIPE).**
   `vc-x1 show-session <file> | head` panics once `head`
   closes the pipe: the logger's `println!` aborts with
   "failed printing to stdout: Broken pipe". Repo-wide
   behavior of the `info!` → `println!` path, but
   show-session (0.70.0-2) is the first subcommand whose
   output routinely feeds a pager/filter. Found during
   0.70.0-2 verification.
   - **Cost:** ugly panic + backtrace hint instead of the
     Unix-conventional silent exit; output before the pipe
     closed is intact.
   - **Fix direction:** handle EPIPE in the logger (write
     via `writeln!` to a locked stdout and exit 0 on
     `BrokenPipe`), or reset SIGPIPE to default on unix at
     startup.

# References

## Bugs

1. **`init --repo local` bare remotes keep HEAD at
   `refs/heads/master`.** The only branch pushed is `main`,
   so a later `jj git clone` of that bare repo has no default
   branch to auto-track and `vc-x1 clone` fails its
   `verify_tracking` check ("bookmark 'main' has non-tracking
   remote 'main@origin'"). Found building `tests/cli_sync.rs`
   (worked around there with `git symbolic-ref HEAD
   refs/heads/main`).
   - **Fix direction:** init's local bare provisioning sets
     HEAD to `refs/heads/main` at creation.

2. **`clone` session-remote derivation mismatches init's
   local naming; relative TARGET breaks the session clone.**
   Two related defects, both found building
   `tests/cli_sync.rs`:
   - `derive_session_url` maps `<x>.git` →
     `<x>.claude.git`, but `init --repo local` names the
     session remote `remote-claude.git` — so a dual clone of

3. **`push` `bookmark-set` races the git index lock.**
   `jj bookmark set` on the colocated work repo failed twice
   (the 0.69.0-3 and 0.69.0-4 pushes, same stage) with

content.text:
`0.70.0-2` work complete. Please review.

What landed:
- `src/show_session.rs` — the command (`ShowSessionArgs` → params → op, `validate-todo` shape) plus the renderer: bold `=== role HH:MM:SS ===` headers that collapse one-line-per-block into visual turns (keyed on role + assistant `message.id`), `[tool] Name: gist` one-liners (Bash → first command line, Read/Write/Edit → file_path, fallback → `key=value` pairs), and a trailing summary line that omits zero clauses. Flags `--thinking`, `--tools` (results indented, capped at 10 lines), `--meta` (meta/system/sidechain).
- `src/show_session/tests.rs` — 7 renderer unit tests; `tests/cli_show_session.rs` — 3 subprocess tests including a truncated-final-line fixture (warns on stderr, exits 0).
- `src/main.rs` — `ShowSession` variant with long_about + dispatch arm (placed after the todo pair); the `-1` temporary module-level `#[allow(dead_code)]` removed.
- `src/transcript.rs` — the module allow was replaced by four targeted type-level `#[allow(dead_code)]`s with a rationale comment: the unrendered fields (uuid threading, `raw`, block ids) are the deliberate forward surface for the linking/index cycles, exercised by tests today.
- `notes/bugs.md` — new bug 4: `println!` panics on EPIPE (`show-session file | head`) — pre-existing logger-wide behavior, surfaced now because this is the first long-output subcommand.
- `Cargo.toml`/`Cargo.lock` — `0.70.0-2`; TODO.md `-2` flipped to done.

Verification: fmt/clippy/full suite green (10 new tests), installed `0.70.0-2`; real-transcript run renders cleanly — the 485-line sample gives `99 turns shown; hidden: 93 tool results, 16 meta/system; skipped: 140 bookkeeping entries` — and the live session file renders exit 0. One data note: all 84 thinking blocks in the sample are empty strings (thinking isn't persisted in this data), so `--thinking` has nothing to reveal there; the empty-skip behavior is per plan.

Convention alert: three new `unwrap_or*`/`unwrap_or` sites in the renderer, all `// OK: obvious` (missing subtype → `"system"`, absent command/file_path in a gist → `""`, short timestamp → raw string).

message.content: I'm thinking we rename show-session to bot-session since we already have a show and we don't have any commands that start with "b", another alternative is session-show then se<tab> will complete the command?

lastPrompt: I'm thinking we rename show-session to bot-session since we already have a show and we don't have any commands that start with "b", another alternative is session-show then se<tab> will complete the c…

content.text:
Good timing — renaming is cheap now, before close-out. My take:

- **The house pattern is verb-noun** (`validate-todo`, `fix-desc`, `squash-push`) — which `show-session` actually follows. But the *display* family breaks that: `list`, `desc`, `chid`, `show` are all named by what they display, not by a verb. So a display command named by its object has equal precedent.
- **`bot-session`** fits that display-family precedent ("the thing shown"), matches the project's established "bot repo" terminology (`validate-bot` already uses `bot` this way — and a session file is exactly bot-repo data), and wins on completion: `b<tab>` is unique while `s` is the most crowded prefix in the CLI (`show`, `symlink`, `sync`, `squash-push`).
- **`session-show`** gets you `se<tab>`… until a second session command exists — `session-list`, `session-index` are the obvious next ones, at which point `se<tab>` stops completing anyway. It also introduces a third naming shape (noun-verb) that nothing else uses. Its one real virtue is grouping: all session commands would sort together in `--help` and share a prefix.

I'd pick **`bot-session`**: unique prefix today, consistent terminology, and if a family grows it extends naturally (`bot-sessions` for the list/index view, or flags on `bot-session`). If you anticipate many session subcommands and value them grouping in help output, `session-show`/`session-list` is the defensible alternative — but `bot-` groups just as well with only two letters' more typing.

Say the word and I'll apply the rename (module, command, strings, tests, ladder/chores titles) as part of `-2` before we commit.

message.content: Yes use bot-session, apply the rename

content.text: Applying the rename across code, tests, and notes:

input.command: mv src/show_session.rs src/bot_session.rs && mv src/show_session src/bot_session && mv tests/cli_show_session.rs tests/cli_bot_session.rs && sed -i -e 's/show_session/bot_session/g' -e 's/ShowSession/BotSession/g' -e 's/show-session/bot-session/g' src/bot_session.rs src/bot_session/tests.rs tests/cli_bot_session.rs src/main.rs && grep -rn "show.session\|ShowSession" src/ tests/ | head

44	/// Banner string emitted as the first line of normal command runs
45	/// and shown at the top of subcommand `--help` output. Built from
46	/// `Cargo.toml`'s name + version at compile time so it stays in
47	/// sync with the bumped version.
48	const BANNER: &str = concat!(env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"), " ", env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"));
49	
50	/// Top-level about line — name, version, and the project tagline
51	/// on a single line. Used as the top-level `about` so `vc-x1 -h`
52	/// reads as one banner-plus-tagline header instead of two stacked
53	/// lines.
54	const TOP_ABOUT: &str = concat!(
55	    env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"),
56	    " ",
57	    env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"),
58	    " - jj workspace tooling"
59	);
60	
61	/// Build the clap command tree with `BANNER` set as `before_help`
62	/// on every subcommand (transitively). Top-level skips `before_help`
63	/// because its own `about` already carries the name+version+tagline.
64	/// Walks via `mut_subcommand` so individual subcommand
65	/// `#[command(long_about = ...)]` blocks don't have to repeat the
66	/// banner text.
67	fn cli_with_banner() -> clap::Command {
68	    fn add_to_subs(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
69	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
70	            .get_subcommands()
71	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
72	            .collect();
73	        for name in names {
74	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
75	        }
76	        cmd
77	    }
78	    fn add_with_banner(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
79	        cmd = cmd.before_help(BANNER);
80	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
81	            .get_subcommands()
82	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
83	            .collect();
84	        for name in names {
85	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
86	        }
87	        cmd
88	    }
89	    add_to_subs(Cli::command())
90	}
91	
92	#[derive(Parser, Debug)]
93	#[command(about = TOP_ABOUT, max_term_width = 80)]
94	pub struct Cli {
95	    /// Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then
96	    /// continue. With no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
97	    ///
98	    /// Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after
99	    /// printing): the banner now rides along with normal
100	    /// subcommand execution rather than gating it, so scripts
101	    /// can capture the version *and* the command's output in
102	    /// one invocation.
103	    #[arg(short = 'V', long = "version", global = true, action = clap::ArgAction::SetTrue)]
104	    pub version: bool,
105	
106	    /// Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace
107	    #[arg(short, long, global = true, action = clap::ArgAction::Count)]
108	    pub verbose: u8,
109	
110	    /// Log file path (captures all levels)
111	    #[arg(long, global = true)]
112	    pub log: Option<std::path::PathBuf>,
113	
114	    #[command(subcommand)]
115	    pub(crate) command: Option<Commands>,
116	}
117

135	        At rest the bot repo's `main` always matches `main@origin` — the\n\
136	        bookmark only moves inside a push / squash-push run, which\n\

66	   `vc-x1 bot-session <file> | head` panics once `head`
67	   closes the pipe: the logger's `println!` aborts with
68	   "failed printing to stdout: Broken pipe". Repo-wide
69	   behavior of the `info!` → `println!` path, but
70	   bot-session (0.70.0-2) is the first subcommand whose
71	   output routinely feeds a pager/filter. Found during

content.text:
Rename applied and verified end-to-end. Updated description for the `-2` commit:

**Title:**
```
feat: bot-session command + conversation renderer
```

**Body:**
```
Work (0.70.0-2): the display half — the bot-session
subcommand (renamed from show-session mid-step: unique b<tab>
prefix, display-family naming, bot-repo terminology; the
pushed -0/-1 titles keep the old stem) renders a transcript
as a conversation. Validated: fmt/clippy clean, full suite
green incl. 10 new tests, installed 0.70.0-2; real-transcript
runs render cleanly, live session file exits 0.

- src/bot_session.rs: new — Args/Params/runner + renderer:
  turn headers collapse per-block lines (role + message.id),
  [tool] one-liner gists (Bash/Read/Write/Edit specialized),
  --thinking/--tools/--meta reveal flags, results capped at
  10 lines, trailing summary line omitting zero clauses
- src/bot_session/tests.rs: new — 7 renderer unit tests
- tests/cli_bot_session.rs: new — 3 subprocess tests incl.
  truncated-final-line fixture (stderr warn, exit 0)
- src/main.rs: BotSession variant + long_about + dispatch,
  placed in the display family after Show; -1's temporary
  module-level #[allow(dead_code)] removed
- src/transcript.rs: targeted type-level #[allow(dead_code)]
  (4 sites, shared rationale comment) for the
  extracted-but-unrendered forward surface (uuid threading,
  raw retention, block ids)
- notes/bugs.md: new bug 4 — info!/println! panics on EPIPE
  (bot-session <file> | head); repo-wide, surfaced by the
  first long-output subcommand
- Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock: version 0.70.0-2
- TODO.md: -2 flipped to done with a rename note; In
  Progress title now bot-session
- notes/chores/chores-13.md: section header synced to
  "feat: bot-session transcript viewer"
```

May I commit and push?

content.text: That's the ANSI bold escape (`ESC[1m`) being written unconditionally — fine on a terminal, garbage in a redirected file. The conventional fix is to emit color only when stdout is a TTY (std's `IsTerminal`, no new dependency). Applying:

message.content: I'd like a way to limit the number of lines;  first/last counts and/or "line offset" / count or other thoughts

content.text:
Good addition — a few design thoughts, then your pick:

**What unit to limit in?** Three candidates:
- **Rendered output lines** — matches your mental model (`head`/`tail` on the output), trivial to implement (slice the rendered `Vec<String>`), and sidesteps the EPIPE bug for the common case.
- **Turns** — the semantic unit ("show me the last 5 exchanges"); often what you actually want when catching up on a session, but a turn's length varies wildly.
- **Source JSONL line numbers** — matches the file (we already track `line_no` per entry); best for correlating with the malformed-line warnings, worst for "just show me the start".

**Flag shape.** Since the fixed summary line always prints, I'd apply limits to the conversation body only, with elision markers (`… (N lines skipped)`) at cut points so a sliced view is self-describing.

questions.question: Which limiting flags should bot-session v1 get?

options.label: --head/--tail/--offset lines (Recommended)
options.description: --head N (first N rendered lines), --tail N (last N), --offset N (skip N first, composes with --head). head+tail together is an error. Elision markers at cut points; summary line always prints.

options.label: --offset/--limit only
options.description: Minimal pair on rendered lines: skip N, then show M. Tail requires knowing the total, so "last N" needs two runs.

options.label: Turn-based --first/--last
options.description: --first N / --last N whole turns (header + its content). Semantic and clean at boundaries, but a turn can be 1 line or 200.

message.content:
I'm thinking --offset O,C
- O = offset
- C = count
- A negative count -C (negative) means goto O-C and display C?
- If only one number and it's positive display that many lines from beginning
- If only one number and it's negative display that many lines from the end

Thoughts?

content.text:
I like it — it's essentially Python slice semantics in one flag, and the single-value forms are exactly `s[:N]` / `s[-N:]`. Let me pin the spec precisely, because the negative-count form has one subtlety worth stating:

**Proposed grammar** (applies to rendered conversation lines; the summary line always prints; elision markers at cut points):

- `--offset O,C` — treat `O` as an **anchor** (0-based, = number of lines skipped):
  - `C` positive: show `C` lines starting at `O` — i.e. `lines[O .. O+C]`.
  - `C` negative: show `|C|` lines **ending at** `O` — i.e. `lines[O-|C| .. O]` ("goto O−C and display C", clamped at 0). So `O` is the forward boundary either way: positive C extends forward from it, negative C extends backward to it.
- `--offset N` (single, positive) — first `N` lines (`lines[..N]`).
- `--offset -N` (single, negative) — last `N` lines (`lines[len-N ..]`).
- Out-of-range anchors clamp; an empty slice just prints the summary.

Two thoughts on top:

1. **Flag name**: since the value carries both offset *and* count, `--lines` might read better than `--offset` — `--lines 100,20`, `--lines 50`, `--lines -50`. Your call; the grammar is identical either way.
2. **Negative O** (e.g. `-100,20` = "start 100 from the end, show 20") falls out naturally of the anchor model, but I'd leave it unspecified-and-rejected in v1 rather than guess — easy to add when a use case appears.

Clap note: needs `allow_hyphen_values` so `--offset -50` parses; that's routine.

Which name do you want — `--offset` or `--lines`? Then I'll implement it into `-2` with unit + CLI tests.

message.content:
The help needs some improving. Actually, we need to update  /README.md to teach
the user what each of the options means. Also we may want to group the output options
together are those: "--thinking, --tools, --meta"

How is --tools different from the "[tool] ..." output

Should we have an --all?

Maybe a --raw which is no header and basically the same as "cat <file-name>" except you can control the --lines?

Thoughts, no changes

content.text:
Thoughts on each, no changes made:

**`--tools` vs `[tool]` — you've spotted a real naming problem.** The `[tool] Bash: cargo test` one-liners are tool *calls* and are always shown (they're part of the assistant's turn). `--tools` reveals tool *results* (what came back, rendered as `[result] …`). So the flag is misnamed — it doesn't control the `[tool]` lines at all. I'd rename it `--results` before this cycle closes; renaming after ship is a breaking change, now it's free.

**Grouping in help — yes, clap does this directly** with `help_heading`: a "Reveal" group (`--thinking`, `--results`, `--meta`) and an "Output range" group (`--lines`). Cheap, and it makes the help self-organizing as flags grow.

**`--all` — yes, worth having.** A shorthand that flips every reveal flag (including future ones) matches what you actually do when digging into a session; the individual flags stay for selective viewing. One decision: `--all` probably should *not* imply `--raw` (below) — it means "reveal everything in the conversation view."

**`--raw` — useful, with one semantic wrinkle worth deciding deliberately.** As you describe it — no rendering, verbatim lines, basically `cat` but honoring `--lines` — the natural unit for `--lines` becomes *source JSONL lines*, not rendered lines. That's actually a feature: raw-mode line numbers then correlate exactly with the malformed-line warnings and with what `jq`/editors see. But it means `--lines` changes meaning depending on mode, which the help must state clearly. Raw mode should probably suppress the summary line too (verbatim means verbatim), or emit it to stderr. Also worth noting `--raw` overlaps with plain `sed -n '10,20p'` — its real value is being one consistent interface plus future-proofing (e.g. raw for a *turn* range once turn addressing exists).

**README** — agreed, and it's already a scheduled obligation: the close-out step includes updating `notes/README.md` for new functionality, and the root README needs a `bot-session` section (flag-by-flag, with a short example of each `--lines` form). Todo #10 (README audit) notes the README drifts silently — best to write this section against the final flag set, i.e. after the rename/grouping decisions land.

**My proposed sequencing**: fold into `-2` the cheap-but-breaking-later items — `--results` rename, help grouping, `--all` — plus the README/notes-README sections at close-out. Defer `--raw` to a follow-on cycle (it's additive, non-breaking, and its `--lines` semantics deserve their own small design pass — could ride with the index view). Want me to proceed with that split?

message.content: Yes proceed with that split

content.text: Proceeding — rename, grouping, `--all` into `-2`; a Todo entry for the deferred `--raw`; README at close-out.

44	/// Banner string emitted as the first line of normal command runs
45	/// and shown at the top of subcommand `--help` output. Built from
46	/// `Cargo.toml`'s name + version at compile time so it stays in
47	/// sync with the bumped version.
48	const BANNER: &str = concat!(env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"), " ", env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"));
49	
50	/// Top-level about line — name, version, and the project tagline
51	/// on a single line. Used as the top-level `about` so `vc-x1 -h`
52	/// reads as one banner-plus-tagline header instead of two stacked
53	/// lines.
54	const TOP_ABOUT: &str = concat!(
55	    env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"),
56	    " ",
57	    env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"),
58	    " - jj workspace tooling"
59	);

61	/// Build the clap command tree with `BANNER` set as `before_help`
62	/// on every subcommand (transitively). Top-level skips `before_help`
63	/// because its own `about` already carries the name+version+tagline.
64	/// Walks via `mut_subcommand` so individual subcommand
65	/// `#[command(long_about = ...)]` blocks don't have to repeat the
66	/// banner text.
67	fn cli_with_banner() -> clap::Command {
68	    fn add_to_subs(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
69	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
70	            .get_subcommands()
71	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
72	            .collect();
73	        for name in names {
74	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
75	        }
76	        cmd
77	    }
78	    fn add_with_banner(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
79	        cmd = cmd.before_help(BANNER);
80	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
81	            .get_subcommands()
82	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
83	            .collect();
84	        for name in names {
85	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
86	        }
87	        cmd
88	    }
89	    add_to_subs(Cli::command())
90	}

content.text:
They are the same — the difference isn't the spec, it's the file. You ran both commands against `2a51d281….jsonl`, which is *this very session's live transcript*, and it grew between your two invocations: 5 seconds apart, 1357 → 1362 rendered lines, 189 → 190 turns, 31 → 32 meta/system. The bot was appending entries (this conversation) while you were looking at it.

Both specs resolve to the identical empty slice with one elision marker and a `0 of N lines shown` summary:

- `--lines 0` → bounds `(len, len)` — empty, one marker.
- `--lines 0,0` → bounds `(0, 0)` — empty, one marker.

Run them back-to-back on any *quiescent* file (e.g. `4f5d498c….jsonl`) and the outputs will be byte-identical.

The plain version: you measured a moving target — the file is the log of the conversation we're having right now, so it got longer between your first and second command. The two flag spellings do exactly the same thing.

Still ready to commit and push `0.70.0-2` on your word.

Applied — the help now groups the flags under `Items listed:` with "Also list …" phrasing, making it clear they add to the always-shown default (prompts, assistant text, `[tool]` calls). All suites green, installed.

In the description, the reveal-flags sub-bullet now reads:

```
  - flags --thinking / --results / --meta / --all, grouped
    under an "Items listed" help heading, add hidden items
    to the default view (prompts, assistant text, and [tool]
    call lines always show); --results reveals [result]
    lines, capped at 10
```

Everything else as last presented. May I commit and push?

message.content:
I'm thinking we add; --headers, --user, --tool, --summary to the Items listed. And only
hardwired default is, --summary.  Then in /.vc-config.toml the user can set their defaults and
we suggest --haders, --user, --tool and --summary as typical. This gives the user complete
control. Plus we add --no-xxx so they have absolute control over everything.

Thoughts?

content.text:
Thoughts — I like the direction (it turns the renderer into a composable item-selection model), with a few design points to settle:

**The item taxonomy needs one more member: `--assistant`.** Your list (`--headers, --user, --tool, --summary` plus the existing `--thinking, --results, --meta`) has no flag for assistant text — under "only `--summary` is hardwired" the assistant's replies would be either unreachable or secretly always-on. The full set falls out naturally: `headers, user, assistant, tool, thinking, results, meta, summary` — eight items, each with `--xxx` / `--no-xxx`, plus `--all` and (I'd add) `--none` as bulk clears, so `--none --user` means "just the prompts" without seven `--no-` flags.

**Flag semantics**: start from the resolved default set, then each flag toggles its item; for a `--xxx`/`--no-xxx` conflict, last-one-wins (clap's `overrides_with` gives this for free). That's the standard, predictable model.

**One gentle pushback on the hardwired default = `--summary` only.** A fresh user (or fresh machine, no config yet) running `vc-x1 bot-session file` would get a single stats line — a confusing first-run experience. I'd make the *built-in* default the typical set (`headers, user, assistant, tool, summary`), with config able to override it to anything, including minimal. Control is identical; only the no-config experience differs. If you specifically want the binary to have near-zero opinion, your version works too — config does the heavy lifting.

**Where the config lives — I'd argue for the user config, not `.vc-config.toml`.** Display preferences are personal taste; `.vc-config.toml` is a *committed, shared* file whose current job is workspace topology (`[workspace] path`), so putting display defaults there imposes one person's taste on every collaborator and mixes concerns. We already have `~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml` loaded into `ctx.user_config` — a `[bot-session]` section there (e.g. `items = ["headers","user","assistant","tool","summary"]`) is the natural home. A repo-level override could come later if a real need appears.

**Sequencing**: `-2` is complete, validated, and reviewed — I'd land it now and make this items/config model the next rung (`0.70.0-3 feat: bot-session item flags + config defaults`) in the same cycle, since it revises flag surface we haven't yet documented in the README (which then gets written once, at close-out, against the final surface).

Want me to proceed that way — push `-2` as approved, then design `-3` with the taxonomy/semantics above (your call on the built-in default and config home)?

message.content: Yes proceed that way, push -2. Your choice for default are fine, but .vc-config.toml is local, although I guess it could be in ~/.config/vc-x1 too and maybe that's already supported. Go on the commit/push

content.text: Pushing `0.70.0-2` with the approved description — stopping once it lands; `-3` design comes next turn.
