message.content: go, start 0.70.2 I'm thinking "raw" rather than being "cat <file>.jsonl" I'm thinking raw allows you to see all possible fields. And some how use that information to track/determine how Anthropic has altered the format so that we might learn what new fields are avialable and see their content. And actually for me to know what's now. Thoughts

content.text:
This reframing makes `--raw` much more valuable — verbatim dump was always the weakest form (it barely beats `cat`/`jq`). What you're describing is **schema exploration**, and we're uniquely positioned for it because the parser already keeps the full `raw` Value per line while the typed layer knows exactly which fields we consume. That difference — *fields present in the data minus fields we model* — is precisely "what's new to learn." My thoughts on the shape:

1. **Field inventory** (the core): walk every line's raw JSON and aggregate dotted paths — `promptSource`, `message.content[].type`, `toolUseResult.stdout`, … — reporting count, value type(s), and a few short sample values per path, broken down by entry type. One command run answers "what does this format actually contain today."

2. **`--unknown` filter**: subtract the paths the typed layer consumes (a `KNOWN_PATHS` list maintained next to the extractor, with a test keeping them honest). What remains is the unexplored surface — your "what's new" view, and the direct answer to *how has Anthropic changed the format* relative to what we understand.

3. **Content drill-down**: once the inventory shows an interesting path, you want the actual JSON — that's where a verbatim/pretty mode still earns its keep: `--raw --lines I,C` printing the selected *source* lines pretty-printed, for inspecting a specific entry.

4. **Drift over time** (follow-on, not 0.70.2): the bot repo keeps every dated transcript, so a committed baseline manifest (or just re-running the inventory across `.claude/*.jsonl` sorted by date) turns this into a change-tracker: "field X first appears 2026-07-02." That wants multi-file scanning, which belongs with the discovery/index cycle.

The plain version: the tool already keeps everything Anthropic writes, even the parts we ignore; this feature counts up every field it sees, flags the ones we don't understand yet, and lets you zoom in on any of them — so format changes surface themselves instead of being discovered by accident.

Scope question for 0.70.2:

options.label: Fields + --unknown + raw drill-down (Recommended)
options.description: The inventory view (--fields), the unknown-fields filter, and pretty-printed --raw --lines for inspecting specific entries. Complete explore loop in one cycle; drift-over-time baseline deferred.

options.label: Fields + --unknown only
options.description: Just the aggregated inventory and the unknown filter. Smallest useful cycle; drill-down via jq until a later cycle adds --raw.

options.label: Everything incl. baseline drift
options.description: Also the multi-file scan and committed baseline manifest for tracking format changes over time. Biggest cycle; pulls discovery/index work forward.

attachment.snippet:
1	//! Claude Code session transcript (`.jsonl`) parsing.
2	//!
3	//! Two-layer design so schema churn in the (undocumented,
4	//! evolving) transcript format never breaks us:
5	//!
6	//! - serde_json is used only as a JSON-text → `Value` parser; the
7	//!   full parsed line is retained in `Entry::raw`, so unknown
8	//!   fields ride along.
9	//! - Hand-written extraction builds the typed layer (`EntryMeta`,
10	//!   `EntryKind`, `ContentBlock`) — every field degrades to
11	//!   `None`/`false`/empty when absent, and unrecognized entry or
12	//!   block types land in `Other` variants instead of erroring.
13	//!
14	//! Scope and footprint, by design:
15	//!
16	//! - `FileTranscript` is one file's parse. A *session* can span
17	//!   several files (sidechain `agent-*.jsonl`, compaction
18	//!   predecessors); assembling those is a later, separate layer.
19	//! - Whole-file in memory: the largest observed file (~8 MB)
20	//!   parses to tens of MB — fine interactively. If that stops
21	//!   holding, streaming via `BufRead` is a drop-in change inside
22	//!   `parse_file`, and `raw` retention could become optional.
23	
24	use serde_json::Value;
25	use std::path::Path;
26	
27	/// One file's parsed transcript: every parseable line, in file order.
28	pub struct FileTranscript {
29	    /// All entries, including ones a conversation view hides.
30	    pub entries: Vec<Entry>,
31	    /// Malformed lines: (1-based line number, parse error text).
32	    /// A live session's truncated final line lands here.
33	    pub malformed: Vec<(usize, String)>,
34	}
35	
36	/// One parsed JSONL line.
37	// dead_code: fields extracted but not yet rendered (uuid
38	// threading, raw retention, block ids) are the typed layer's
39	// forward surface for later cycles (linking, index view,
40	// cross-file refs); unit tests exercise them today.
41	#[allow(dead_code)]
42	pub struct Entry {
43	    /// 1-based line number in the source file.
44	    pub line_no: usize,
45	    /// Common metadata shared across entry types.
46	    pub meta: EntryMeta,
47	    /// Classified payload.
48	    pub kind: EntryKind,
49	    /// The full original parsed line; unknown fields ride along.
50	    pub raw: Value,
51	}
52	
53	/// Common top-level fields; each degrades gracefully when absent.
54	// dead_code: see the Entry note.
55	#[allow(dead_code)]
56	pub struct EntryMeta {
57	    /// Unique id of this entry.
58	    pub uuid: Option<String>,
59	    /// Id of the parent entry (conversation threading).
60	    pub parent_uuid: Option<String>,
61	    /// Raw ISO-8601 timestamp string, unparsed.
62	    pub timestamp: Option<String>,
63	    /// Session id (`sessionId`, falling back to `session_id`).
64	    pub session_id: Option<String>,
65	    /// True on subagent (sidechain) lines.
66	    pub is_sidechain: bool,
67	    /// True on injected/meta lines (not typed by the user).
68	    pub is_meta: bool,
69	    /// Working directory at the time of the entry.
70	    pub cwd: Option<String>,
71	}
72	
73	/// Classified payload by top-level `type`.
74	// dead_code: see the Entry note.
75	#[allow(dead_code)]
76	pub enum EntryKind {
77	    /// `type=user`: a typed prompt (string content) or tool
78	    /// results (array content) — both normalized to blocks.
79	    User {
80	        /// Normalized content blocks.
81	        content: Vec<ContentBlock>,
82	        /// `promptSource` — "typed" marks a real human prompt.
83	        prompt_source: Option<String>,
84	    },
85	    /// `type=assistant`: in practice one JSONL line per content
86	    /// block, modeled as a `Vec` to stay shape-agnostic.
87	    Assistant {
88	        /// Normalized content blocks.
89	        content: Vec<ContentBlock>,
90	        /// `message.id` — groups the blocks of one API turn.
91	        message_id: Option<String>,
92	    },
93	    /// `type=system` bookkeeping (e.g. subtype `turn_duration`).
94	    System {
95	        /// The `subtype` field, when present.
96	        subtype: Option<String>,
97	    },
98	    /// Every other `type` (mode, permission-mode, progress,
99	    /// file-history-*, attachment, unknown/future types, …).
100	    Other {
101	        /// The original `type` value ("<none>" when absent).
102	        entry_type: String,
103	    },
104	}
105	
106	/// One block of `message.content`.
107	// dead_code: see the Entry note.
108	#[allow(dead_code)]
109	pub enum ContentBlock {
110	    /// Visible text.
111	    Text {
112	        /// The text body.
113	        text: String,
114	    },
115	    /// Extended-thinking text.
116	    Thinking {
117	        /// The thinking body.
118	        thinking: String,
119	    },
120	    /// A tool invocation.
121	    ToolUse {
122	        /// Tool-use id (pairs with a later `ToolResult`).
123	        id: String,
124	        /// Tool name.
125	        name: String,
126	        /// Tool input, kept as raw JSON.
127	        input: Value,
128	    },
129	    /// A tool's result, sent back on a user line.
130	    ToolResult {
131	        /// The `tool_use_id` this result answers.
132	        tool_use_id: String,
133	        /// Result text (string form, or array-of-text joined).
134	        text: String,
135	        /// True when the tool errored.
136	        is_error: bool,
137	    },
138	    /// Unrecognized block type — counted, never rendered.
139	    Other {
140	        /// The original block `type` value.
141	        block_type: String,
142	    },
143	}
144	
145	/// Parse a whole transcript file.
146	///
147	/// - Errors only on I/O (unreadable file).
148	/// - Malformed lines are recorded in `FileTranscript::malformed`.
149	pub fn parse_file(path: &Path) -> Result<FileTranscript, Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
150	    let text = std::fs::read_to_string(path)
151	        .map_err(|e| format!("cannot read {}: {e}", path.display()))?;
152	    Ok(parse_str(&text))
153	}
154	
155	/// Parse transcript text (unit-testable without files).
156	///
157	/// - Blank lines are skipped.
158	/// - Each remaining line becomes an `Entry` or a `malformed`
159	///   record; parsing never fails as a whole.
160	pub fn parse_str(text: &str) -> FileTranscript {
161	    let mut entries = Vec::new();
162	    let mut malformed = Vec::new();
163	    for (i, line) in text.lines().enumerate() {
164	        let line_no = i + 1;
165	        if line.trim().is_empty() {
166	            continue;
167	        }
168	        match parse_line(line_no, line) {
169	            Ok(entry) => entries.push(entry),
170	            Err(err) => malformed.push((line_no, err)),
171	        }
172	    }
173	    FileTranscript { entries, malformed }
174	}
175	
176	/// Parse one JSONL line into an `Entry`.
177	///
178	/// - Err on invalid JSON or a non-object top level.
179	/// - Never panics; missing fields degrade per the type docs.
180	fn parse_line(line_no: usize, line: &str) -> Result<Entry, String> {
181	    let raw: Value = serde_json::from_str(line).map_err(|e| e.to_string())?;
182	    if !raw.is_object() {
183	        return Err("top level is not a JSON object".to_string());
184	    }
185	    let meta = extract_meta(&raw);
186	    let kind = extract_kind(&raw);
187	    Ok(Entry {
188	        line_no,
189	        meta,
190	        kind,
191	        raw,
192	    })
193	}
194	
195	/// Extract the common top-level fields.
196	fn extract_meta(v: &Value) -> EntryMeta {
197	    EntryMeta {
198	        uuid: get_str(v, "uuid"),
199	        parent_uuid: get_str(v, "parentUuid"),
200	        timestamp: get_str(v, "timestamp"),
201	        session_id: get_str(v, "sessionId").or_else(|| get_str(v, "session_id")),
202	        is_sidechain: get_bool(v, "isSidechain"),
203	        is_meta: get_bool(v, "isMeta"),
204	        cwd: get_str(v, "cwd"),
205	    }
206	}
207	
208	/// Classify the line by its top-level `type` field.
209	fn extract_kind(v: &Value) -> EntryKind {
210	    let entry_type = get_str(v, "type").unwrap_or_else(|| "<none>".to_string());
211	    match entry_type.as_str() {

213	            content: extract_content(&v["message"]),
214	            prompt_source: get_str(v, "promptSource"),
215	        },

1	//! The `bot-session` subcommand: display a Claude Code session
2	//! transcript (`.jsonl`) as a readable conversation.
3	//!
4	//! Output is a set of *items* — headers, user, assistant, tool,
5	//! thinking, results, meta, summary — each toggled by `--<item>` /
6	//! `--no-<item>` flags (last one wins), with `--all` / `--none` as
7	//! bulk bases. The default set (headers, user, assistant, tool,
8	//! summary) can be replaced by the user config's
9	//! `[bot-session].items` list; CLI flags then adjust the resolved
10	//! set. Bookkeeping entry types are never rendered.
11	//! A trailing summary line always reports what was hidden or
12	//! skipped. Malformed lines (e.g. a live session's truncated
13	//! last line) warn to stderr and never fail the run.
14	
15	use std::path::PathBuf;
16	
17	use clap::Args;
18	use log::{debug, info, warn};
19	use serde_json::Value;
20	
21	use crate::context::Context;
22	use crate::subcommand::SubcommandRunner;
23	use crate::transcript::{self, ContentBlock, EntryKind, FileTranscript};
24	
25	/// Default max lines of one tool result shown under `--results`.

27

40	        long,

42	        overrides_with = "no_summary"
43	    )]
44	    pub summary: bool,
45	    /// Do not list the trailing summary line
46	    #[arg(
47	        long,
48	        hide = true,
49	        help_heading = "Items listed (--no-<item> to disable)",
50	        overrides_with = "summary"
51	    )]
52	    pub no_summary: bool,

54	    /// List turn headers (=== role time ===) [default: on]

184	    /// Limit output to a slice of the rendered conversation
185	    /// lines (Note: Index is 0-based):
186	    ///   N    — first N lines (0 = summary only)
187	    ///   -N   — last N lines
188	    ///   I,C  — C lines starting at Index I

209	
210	    /// Field inventory instead of the conversation: every dotted

214	    pub fields: bool,

"""Cut points show an `… (N lines skipped)` marker, and a sliced
run's summary leads with `K of M lines shown` so it never
claims more than was displayed. Timestamps are shown as UTC
(`Z`) exactly when the source timestamp carries it — observed
always, but the format is undocumented, so anything else would
pass through verbatim.

**Alternate views** — the transcript format is undocumented
and evolves, so bot-session doubles as a schema explorer. The
parser keeps every field Anthropic writes while the typed
layer consumes a known subset; the difference is the
unexplored surface:

| Flag | View |
|------|------|
| `--fields` | Field inventory: every dotted path per entry type, with count, value kinds, and short samples |
| `--unknown` | Like `--fields`, but only paths the typed layer does not consume — the unmodeled / new surface |
| `--raw` | Pretty-printed source lines (unparseable lines pass through verbatim); no summary or markers |

In `--raw`, `--lines` selects **source JSONL lines** (what
jq/editors count), not rendered lines. `--fields`/`--unknown`
ignore item flags; `--raw` conflicts with them.

```
# What does this format actually contain today?
vc-x1 bot-session --fields FILE

# What don't we model yet / what did Anthropic add?
vc-x1 bot-session --unknown FILE

# Inspect one entry in full (source line 42)
vc-x1 bot-session --raw --lines 42,1 FILE
```""")
open('README.md', 'w').write(s)

s = open('src/main.rs').read()

        --unknown (only unmodeled paths — how the format moved), and\\n\\
        --raw (pretty-printed source lines; --lines then selects\\n\\
        source JSONL lines).\"""")
open('src/main.rs', 'w').write(s)
EOF

Commits:

(barely beats jq) to *schema exploration*: the parser keeps

known subset — the difference is the unexplored surface, so
format changes surface themselves instead of being discovered
by accident.

  per entry type ( `[]` marks array elements) with count,
  value kinds, and up to 3 short samples.

  First real run: 132 unknown paths in one 485-line file
  (message.model, stop_reason, thinking signatures,
  content[].caller.*, requestId, userType, …).
- `--raw [--lines]`: pretty-printed source lines for content
  drill-down; `--lines` unit is *source JSONL lines* here
  (matches jq/editors and the malformed-line warnings);
  unparseable lines pass through verbatim; no summary, no
  markers. Conflicts with `--fields`.

  territory.""")

content.text:
`0.70.2` work complete. Please review.

What landed (single-commit cycle):
- `src/transcript.rs` — the exploration primitives: `leaf_paths` (dotted-path walker, `[]` for array elements, empty containers count as leaves), `KNOWN_PATHS` (the extractor's consumed set) and `is_known` with subtree semantics (`message.content[].input` covers everything beneath it).
- `src/bot_session.rs` — "Alternate views" flag group and dispatch:
  - `--fields`: per-entry-type inventory — path, value kinds, count, up to 3 short samples, aligned columns, `N paths across M entries` summary.
  - `--unknown`: same view filtered to unmodeled paths. First real run on the 485-line sample: **132 unknown paths** — `message.model`, `stop_reason`, thinking `signature`s, `content[].caller.*`, `requestId`, `userType`, the full shape of every bookkeeping type.
  - `--raw`: pretty-printed *source* lines; `--lines` switches to source-JSONL-line units (matches jq and the malformed warnings); unparseable lines pass through verbatim; no summary/markers; conflicts with `--fields`.
- Tests — unit: `leaf_paths_walks`, `is_known_subtrees` (incl. the `uuidX` non-match edge); CLI: `cli_bot_session_alternate_views` (fields shows known+unknown, unknown filters, raw pretty-prints line 1, the truncated last line passes through verbatim, `--raw --fields` rejected by clap).
- `README.md` — Alternate views subsection with the three-row table, the source-line-unit warning, and the three worked questions ("what does the format contain / what don't we model / inspect one entry"). `src/main.rs` long_about extended.
- Bookkeeping — ladder flipped, Done entry `[[5]]`, chores section filled (empty `Commits:` per timing); `Cargo.toml`/`Cargo.lock` `0.70.2`, installed.

Validation: fmt/clippy clean, all 6 suites green, `vc-x1 -V` = 0.70.2, all three views exercised on real transcripts.

One design note: `--fields`/`--unknown` ignore the item flags rather than conflicting (documented in README); only `--raw` hard-conflicts. Ready for the description when you are.

content.text:
Good catch — resolved. The rule is now uniform: **in all three alternate views `--lines` selects source JSONL lines**; `--fields`/`--unknown` aggregate only the selected lines, and a sliced inventory's summary says so explicitly. A CLI test pins `--fields --lines 1,1` (only the prompt line's fields, `across 1 entries`, the selected-of note). README, long_about, chores, and the Done entry all state the shared unit rule.

Everything validated: fmt/clippy clean, all 6 suites green (the new assertion included), installed, your exact command verified.

Ready for the description review whenever you are.

The clean rule is **`--lines` means source JSONL lines in every view** — it's also more stable (a rendered-line slice selects different conversation regions depending on which items are on). 1	//! The `bot-session` subcommand: display a Claude Code session
2	//! transcript (`.jsonl`) as a readable conversation.
3	//!
4	//! Output is a set of *items* — headers, user, assistant, tool,
5	//! thinking, results, meta, summary — each toggled by `--<item>` /
6	//! `--no-<item>` flags (last one wins), with `--all` / `--none` as
7	//! bulk bases. The default set (headers, user, assistant, tool,
8	//! summary) can be replaced by the user config's
9	//! `[bot-session].items` list; CLI flags then adjust the resolved
10	//! set. Bookkeeping entry types are never rendered.
11	//! A trailing summary line always reports what was hidden or
12	//! skipped. Malformed lines (e.g. a live session's truncated
13	//! last line) warn to stderr and never fail the run.

42	use crate::subcommand::SubcommandRunner;
43	
44	/// Banner string emitted as the first line of normal command runs
45	/// and shown at the top of subcommand `--help` output. Built from
46	/// `Cargo.toml`'s name + version at compile time so it stays in
47	/// sync with the bumped version.
48	const BANNER: &str = concat!(env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"), " ", env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"));
49	
50	/// Top-level about line — name, version, and the project tagline
51	/// on a single line. Used as the top-level `about` so `vc-x1 -h`
52	/// reads as one banner-plus-tagline header instead of two stacked
53	/// lines.
54	const TOP_ABOUT: &str = concat!(
55	    env!("CARGO_PKG_NAME"),
56	    " ",
57	    env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"),
58	    " - jj workspace tooling"
59	);
60	
61	/// Build the clap command tree with `BANNER` set as `before_help`
62	/// on every subcommand (transitively). Top-level skips `before_help`
63	/// because its own `about` already carries the name+version+tagline.
64	/// Walks via `mut_subcommand` so individual subcommand
65	/// `#[command(long_about = ...)]` blocks don't have to repeat the
66	/// banner text.
67	fn cli_with_banner() -> clap::Command {
68	    fn add_to_subs(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
69	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
70	            .get_subcommands()
71	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
72	            .collect();
73	        for name in names {
74	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
75	        }
76	        cmd
77	    }
78	    fn add_with_banner(mut cmd: clap::Command) -> clap::Command {
79	        cmd = cmd.before_help(BANNER);
80	        let names: Vec<String> = cmd
81	            .get_subcommands()
82	            .map(|c| c.get_name().to_string())
83	            .collect();
84	        for name in names {
85	            cmd = cmd.mut_subcommand(name, add_with_banner);
86	        }
87	        cmd
88	    }
89	    add_to_subs(Cli::command())
90	}
91	
92	#[derive(Parser, Debug)]
93	#[command(about = TOP_ABOUT, max_term_width = 80)]
94	pub struct Cli {
95	    /// Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then
96	    /// continue. With no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
97	    ///
98	    /// Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after
99	    /// printing): the banner now rides along with normal
100	    /// subcommand execution rather than gating it, so scripts
101	    /// can capture the version *and* the command's output in
102	    /// one invocation.
103	    #[arg(short = 'V', long = "version", global = true, action = clap::ArgAction::SetTrue)]
104	    pub version: bool,
105	
106	    /// Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace
107	    #[arg(short, long, global = true, action = clap::ArgAction::Count)]
108	    pub verbose: u8,
109	
110	    /// Log file path (captures all levels)
111	    #[arg(long, global = true)]
112	    pub log: Option<std::path::PathBuf>,

content.text:
`--lines` is now one concept: **source JSONL lines, every view**. The conversation view renders whichever entries fall in the slice (with `… (N source lines skipped)` markers and a `--lines selected K of L source lines` summary clause whose stats describe only the slice); `--fields`/`--unknown` inventory the slice; `--raw` prints it. Your `--lines 0,10` example now shows exactly the region's conversation — 1 turn, 2 meta hidden, 4 bookkeeping skipped.

Consequences worth noting from the refactor: the old rendered-line slicer (`apply_lines`) and its "N of M lines shown / full render" summary are gone; `transcript::parse_file` lost its last caller and was removed (each view reads the file text itself — needed for the total line count). "Show me the last 15 *output* lines" is what `| head`/`| tail` pipes are for (modulo the EPIPE bug 4).

Validation: fmt/clippy clean, all 6 suites green (the `--lines` CLI test rewritten for source units, a `render_source_slice` unit test added, `line_bounds` cases pinned directly), installed; README/long_about/chores/Done all state the single-unit rule.

Ready for the description review.

content.text:
Yes — exactly right. The slice (`--lines`) selects *which source lines are considered*; the view then decides what to *show* from them:

- `--fields` inventories everything in the slice, bookkeeping included — that's its job as the schema explorer, so line 1's `mode` entry appears.
- The conversation view applies the item filter to the same slice — `mode` and its kin are never rendered (no item covers bookkeeping), so they land in the summary as `skipped: 8 bookkeeping entries`, and the first *renderable* entry in the slice is your prompt.

The summary accounts for it: 10 source lines selected → 1 turn shown + 8 bookkeeping skipped + 1 line that renders nothing silently (an assistant line whose thinking block is empty — this file's thinking is all empty strings, and empty thinking is skipped without counting, per the 0.70.0 design).

So: same slice, different lenses — `--fields`/`--raw` are "what's in these lines," the conversation view is "what's worth reading in these lines." If you'd rather the summary also count silently-skipped empties so every selected line is accounted for, that's a small tweak — say the word. Otherwise, the description stands as presented — may I commit and push?

That paste looks like a re-send of the earlier (pre-fix) output — its summary shows the old format (`10 of 2695 lines shown (--lines); full render: 373 turns`), timestamped 04:19, *before* the unification landed. Your own runs at 04:26 and 04:30 show the current installed behavior, which is what you expected:

```
=== user 2026-07-17 16:09:56Z ===
<command-name>/effort</command-name> …
… (1718 source lines skipped)

bot-session: 1 turns shown; hidden: 2 meta/system; skipped: 4 bookkeeping entries; --lines selected 10 of 1728 source lines
```

i.e. the conversation view now renders exactly the entries within source lines 0–10 and the stats describe only that slice — consistent with `--raw` and `--fields`.

If you meant something further — for instance that the conversation view should also *render* bookkeeping entries somehow rather than counting them as skipped — tell me and we'll design it. Otherwise everything you've observed is the intended, current behavior, and the `0.70.2` description stands as presented: may I commit and push?

attachment.snippet:
1	# vc-x1
2	
3	- [Overview](#vc-x1)
4	- [Architecture](ARCHITECTURE.md)
5	- [Terminology](#terminology)
6	- [Usage](#usage)
7	  - [Revision shortcuts](#revision-shortcuts)
8	  - [Shell completion](#shell-completion)
9	  - [validate-desc](#validate-desc)
10	  - [fix-desc](#fix-desc)
11	  - [validate-todo](#validate-todo)
12	  - [fix-todo](#fix-todo)
13	  - [validate-bot](#validate-bot)
14	  - [clone](#clone)
15	  - [init](#init)
16	  - [symlink](#symlink)
17	  - [sync](#sync)
18	  - [revert](#revert)
19	  - [squash-push](#squash-push)
20	  - [push](#push)
21	  - [Testing push + squash-push](#testing-push--squash-push)
22	  - [Testing the ochid-trailer guard](#testing-the-ochid-trailer-guard)
23	- [Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers](#cross-repo-linking-with-git-trailers)
24	- [jj Tips for Git Users](#jj-tips-for-git-users)
25	- [Thoughts for the future](#thoughts-for-the-future)
26	- [Contributing](#contributing)
27	- [License](#license)
28	
29	This is experiment 1 to explore creating a Vibe Coding (vc) environment.
30	We will investigate ways of using the dual jj-git repo concept, explored
31	in [hw-jjg-bot](https://github.com/winksaville/hw-jjg-bot.git) to
32	initially make it easy to see how the code base evolved. This is
33	made possible by the fact that we have two repos one with the code
34	and one with the conversation with the bot.
35	
36	I've chosen the jj-git environment because jj provides the concept that
37	each commit has an immutable changeID as well as the mutable commitID
38	of git. The idea is that each commit made on repo A writes the
39	changeID in the commit message to repo B. Thus there is a cross reference
40	between the two repos and this will allow vc-x1 to show how the repo
41	evolved and the entity (bot or human) can more clearly understand **how** and
42	most importantly **why** the code evolved.
43	
44	The solution space is wide open, from trivial CLI, web or app based
45	(mobile/non-mobile). In addition, I could see this as an extension to
46	existing programming editors like vscode and zed or even creating our
47	own IDE for vc.
48	
49	See [Initial commit with dual jj-git repos](./notes/chores/chores-01.md#initial-commit-with-dual-jj-git-repos)
50	for how the initial commit was created with the dual jj-git repos. After
51	doing so and I then created this README.md file.
52	
53	## Terminology
54	
55	A vc-x1 workspace pairs two jj-git repos, and this README names
56	their two sides **work** and **bot**:
57	
58	- **work repo** — the repo at the project root, holding the work
59	  product itself (source code, docs — the repo you would have
60	  anyway).
61	- **bot repo** — the `.claude` repo nested inside it, holding the
62	  bot's session data (the conversation that produced the work).
63	- **work** / **bot** — the two sides as roles, used wherever a
64	  command or doc needs to say which repo it means (e.g. push's
65	  `commit-work` / `commit-bot` stages).
66	
67	One historical wrinkle: the `-s`/`--scope` flag still spells the
68	work side `code` (`-s code`, `-s code,bot`) — that keyword predates
69	the work/bot naming.
70	
71	## Usage
72	
73	```
74	vc-x1 list [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # List commits in a jj repo
75	vc-x1 desc [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Show full description of a commit
76	vc-x1 chid [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Print changeID(s) for a revision
77	vc-x1 show [-r REVISION] [-n COMMITS]  # Show commit details and diff summary
78	vc-x1 bot-session <FILE> [OPTS]        # Display a session transcript as a conversation
79	vc-x1 validate-desc [OPTS]                 # Validate commit descriptions
80	vc-x1 fix-desc [OPTS]                     # Fix commit descriptions (dry-run default)
81	vc-x1 validate-todo [FILE]                # Check todo-file entry numbering
82	vc-x1 fix-todo [FILE]                     # Renumber todo file (dry-run default)
83	vc-x1 validate-bot [OPTS]                 # Check the bot repo is published
84	vc-x1 clone <REPO> [NAME] [OPTS]          # Clone a dual-repo project
85	vc-x1 init <TARGET> [OPTS]                # Create a new dual-repo project
86	vc-x1 symlink [TARGET] [OPTS]             # Create Claude Code project symlink
87	vc-x1 sync [OPTS]                          # Fetch + sync both repos to their remotes
88	vc-x1 revert [OPTS]                        # Restore repos to a prior operation
89	vc-x1 squash-push [BOOKMARK] [OPTS]        # Squash @ into @-, advance a bookmark, push
90	vc-x1 push [BOOKMARK] [OPTS]               # Commit both repos, push work, squash-push bot
91	vc-x1 --version                            # Print version
92	vc-x1 --help                           # Print help
93	```
94	
95	**The `..` notation:** dots on the revision show which direction to
96	list commits:
97	
98	- `x..` — x at top, ancestors below (older commits)
99	- `..x` — descendants above (newer commits), x at bottom
100	- `..x..` — both directions, x in the middle
101	
102	COMMITS is the total number of commits to show (x is always included).
103	Without dots, `x` with a count defaults to `x..` (ancestors).
104	For `..x..`, the budget is split: ancestors get the extra on odd counts.
105	
106	```
107	vc-x1 list -r @              # just @ (1 commit)
108	vc-x1 list -r @.. -n 5       # @ + 4 ancestors (5 commits, @ at top)
109	vc-x1 list -r ..@ -n 3       # 2 descendants + @ (3 commits, @ at bottom)
110	vc-x1 list -r ..@.. -n 3     # 1 descendant, @, 1 ancestor (3 commits)
111	vc-x1 list -r ..@.. -n 4     # 1 descendant, @, 2 ancestors (4 commits)
112	```
113	
114	### Shell completion
115	
116	vc-x1 provides tab completion for all subcommands and flags using claps
117	[unstable-dynamic](https://docs.rs/clap_complete/latest/clap_complete/env/struct.CompleteEnv.html)
118	only feature. It is a "simple" implementation and does not handle completing
119	revisions but still useful. To enable it, add one of the following to your shell's startup file:
120	
121	```bash
122	# bash (~/.bashrc)
123	source <(COMPLETE=bash vc-x1)
124	
125	# zsh (~/.zshrc)
126	source <(COMPLETE=zsh vc-x1)
127	
128	# fish (~/.config/fish/config.fish)
129	source (COMPLETE=fish vc-x1 | psub)
130	```
131	
132	Completions are generated dynamically by the binary, so they stay in
133	sync with the installed version automatically.
134	
135	### Positional shorthand
136	
137	REVISION and COMMITS can be given as positional arguments, omitting
138	`-r` and `-n`:
139	
140	```
141	vc-x1 list x                # just x (1 commit)
142	vc-x1 list x 5              # x.. 5 → x + 4 ancestors (5 commits)
143	vc-x1 list x.. 5            # same as above
144	vc-x1 list ..x 3            # 2 descendants + x (3 commits)
145	vc-x1 list ..x.. 3          # 1 descendant, x, 1 ancestor (3 commits)
146	vc-x1 list ..x.. 1          # just x (1 commit)
147	```
148	
149	**Defaults:** with no arguments, REVISION is `@` and COMMITS is 1
150	(just the working copy).
151	
152	Named flags `-r`/`--revision`, `-n`/`--commits`, `-R`/`--repo`, and
153	`-s`/`--scope` take precedence over positional arguments.
154	
155	### Multi-repo queries
156	
157	`-s`/`--scope` selects workspace sides by role keyword; `code` is the
158	work repo, `bot` is the `.claude` repo, `code,bot` is both. The
159	workspace root is found by walking up from cwd (the existing
160	`find_workspace_root` rule).
161	
162	```
163	vc-x1 chid -s code,bot          # both sides of the workspace
164	vc-x1 chid -s code              # just the work repo
165	vc-x1 chid -s bot               # just the bot repo
166	vc-x1 list @.. 3 -s code,bot    # works with chid / desc / list / show
167	```
168	
169	`-R`/`--repo` takes a single path. Used alone it points at one jj
170	repo (workspace lookup is skipped); combined with `-s` it overrides
171	the workspace root for the role lookup:
172	
173	```
174	vc-x1 chid -R .                  # single repo at .
175	vc-x1 chid -R .claude            # single repo at .claude
176	vc-x1 chid -R ../other -s code,bot   # both sides of ../other workspace
177	```
178	
179	Defaults preserve prior behavior — no flag → `[.]`, `-R foo` alone
180	→ `[foo]`. When multiple repos are resolved (any `-s` that names
181	more than one role), output is labeled with bold `=== path ===`

1	# Chores-13
2	
3	Continuation of `chores-12.md` (closed at `0.62.x`). This
4	file covers the `0.63.0` cycle onward.
5	
6	Subsection headers that record a release use the
7	trailing-version format from AGENTS.md: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.
8	Reference numbering is file-local — see
9	[`AGENTS.md`](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering); chores-13
10	starts at `[1]`.
11	
12	## docs: adopt AGENTS.md (0.63.0)
13	
14	Commits: [[1]]
15	
16	The bot-instructions file is renamed `CLAUDE.md` → `AGENTS.md`
17	so Zed and the broader agent-tooling ecosystem (which now
18	default to `AGENTS.md`) read it natively. Claude Code still
19	auto-loads only `CLAUDE.md`, so a one-line `CLAUDE.md` import
20	shim (`@AGENTS.md`) keeps it working — both tools read one
21	canonical file.
22	
23	- **Single canonical file** — `AGENTS.md` holds the content;
24	  `CLAUDE.md` is a one-line `@AGENTS.md` import for Claude
25	  Code's loader. Nothing human-facing points at the shim.
26	- **Live references repointed** — every `CLAUDE.md` mention
27	  and `#anchor` link in live docs (`README.md`,
28	  `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/`, the `src/push.rs` pre-commit
29	  doc comment, and `AGENTS.md` itself) now names `AGENTS.md`,
30	  so links resolve in editors and on GitHub.
31	- **History left as written** — `CLAUDE.md` prose in
32	  `chores-01..12` and `done.md` records what was true at the
33	  time and is untouched; only the live navigational anchor
34	  links in the `chores-10/11/12` headers
35	  (`#reference-numbering`) were repointed so they don't
36	  dangle against the one-line `CLAUDE.md` shim.
37	
38	### Why an import shim, not a symlink
39	
40	A symlink `CLAUDE.md → AGENTS.md` also satisfies Claude
41	Code's loader, but the `@AGENTS.md` import was chosen
42	because it is explicit and visible in a diff and does not
43	depend on symlink semantics (git symlink handling,
44	cross-platform checkout). Separately, GitHub renders a
45	symlinked `.md` as a stub page rather than the target's
46	markdown, so a `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link through a symlink
47	would not resolve — moot here since live links now point
48	straight at `AGENTS.md`, but it reinforces keeping
49	`CLAUDE.md` a real file.
50	
51	## docs: tighten after-finalize rule (0.63.1)
52	
53	Commits: [[2]]
54	
55	The stop-and-wait rule in `cycle-protocol.md` was titled
56	`### After finalize: stop and wait` and phrased as "final
57	words go in the approval prompt *before* executing finalize"
58	— wording that assumes finalize is a separate,
59	manually-invoked step and names only finalize as the
60	trigger. With `vc-x1 push` the push and `vc-x1 finalize` on
61	`.claude` are bundled as the tail stages, so there is no
62	"before finalize" gap left to speak in once the push is
63	invoked. After `0.63.0`'s push the bot emitted a closing
64	summary *after* `vc-x1 push` returned, violating the rule
65	(and likely riding into the `.claude` commit via the
66	`--delay 10` window).
67	
68	- **Renamed to "After push or finalize"** — both triggers
69	  are named, in the title and the paragraph: the remote
70	  crossing is the push itself, and `vc-x1 finalize` on
71	  `.claude` is the detached step. This also covers a manual
72	  `jj git push` (push, no finalize) and a manual
73	  `vc-x1 finalize` (no wrapper).
74	- **Wrapper case spelled out** — `vc-x1 push` does both as
75	  tail stages (`push-app`, then `vc-x1 finalize` on
76	  `.claude`): closing words go *before* invoking the wrapper,
77	  and the bot does not purposely emit anything after it
78	  returns. The rule names the `vc-x1 finalize` operation, not
79	  the wrapper's internal `finalize-claude` stage label.
80	
81	The bot thinks the original lapse was a compliance slip (the
82	rule was read and quoted, then broken), not a comprehension
83	gap; the doc change closes the one genuine ambiguity (the
84	wrapper bundles push + finalize, leaving no gap to speak in)
85	rather than expecting prose alone to prevent the slip. The
86	rule lives only in `cycle-protocol.md`; AGENTS.md already
87	mandates reading it before commit work, so no duplicate
88	restatement was added there.
89	
90	## docs: codify merge-non-ff recipe (0.64.0)
91	
92	Commits: [[3]]
93	
94	Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
95	but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
96	AGENTS.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
97	manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
98	standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
99	broader cycle-protocol.md codification).
100	
101	- Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
102	  --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk → first
103	  parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
104	  merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
105	- Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
106	  `-d` alias — update AGENTS.md's `-d` spellings (jj
107	  Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`.
108	- `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
109	  `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
110	  AGENTS.md).
111	- Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
112	  keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
113	  `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
114	  `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
115	  before the close-out push.
116	
117	### As-built ladder
118	
119	- 0.64.0-0 Preparation — version bump, 0.63.1 `Commits:`
120	  backfill, item pickup. Also clarified the Preparation
121	  step itself: a Cargo.lock-update bullet, reworded the
122	  Todo "move into In Progress", and a Prose-form intro
123	  line at the top of `cycle-protocol.md`.
124	- 0.64.0-1 cycle-protocol.md — promote the recipe to a
125	  `### Merge non-ff recipe` subsection (rebase → `jj new`
126	  lift → push, with the parents broken out as sub-bullets
127	  and a post-hoc caveat); trim the shape bullet to point
128	  at it. Also reword `### Shape at close-out push`: the
129	  intro reframed (work done, shape chosen at push,
130	  post-publish change is a remote rewrite) and Merge
131	  non-ff tagged as the current default.
132	- 0.64.0-2 AGENTS.md — standardize `-d` → `--onto` in the
133	  post-amend note; add a jj Basics bullet for the
134	  `--onto`/`-o` spelling; sub-bullet the post-amend
135	  command list; reframe the note around jj's empty-`@`
136	  final form, dropping the merge-specific example and the
137	  HEAD-detach mechanism (the recipe now owns the merge
138	  sequence).
139	- 0.64.0-3 review round — make `### Merge non-ff recipe`
140	  step 2 self-contained (own the empty-`@` why inline) and
141	  drop the AGENTS.md post-amend `jj new` note entirely. The
142	  note's non-merge cases proved mis-categorized: `jj squash
143	  --into` leaves `@` clean on top, and `jj edit` lands `@`
144	  on the commit deliberately (you `jj new` when done, not
145	  to restore). One-directional ref (Shape → recipe), no
146	  circle.
147	
148	## docs: record finalize ochid-loss bug (0.65.1)
149	
150	Commits: [[4]]
151	
152	The fc project hit a `finalize` squash that silently dropped a
153	described journal's message and its 6 `ochid:` trailers
154	(2026-06-08; recovered 2026-06-10 via op-log surgery). The bug
155	write-up and two doc-link fixes sat uncommitted in the working
156	copy; this cycle lands them, ports fc's AGENTS.md additions
157	back, and queues the fix as Todo #1.
158	
159	- bugs.md gains the incident as Bugs #1: the failure mode
160	  (finalize's `--use-destination-message` assumption inverts
161	  when the journal is described on `@`), the cost (dangling
162	  code-side ochids), and the fix direction (detect source-only
163	  `ochid:` trailers; refuse or merge instead of dropping).
164	- fc additions ported back:
165	  - AGENTS.md: use-jj-not-git (jj Basics intro) and
166	    one-command-per-shell-invocation (Working Directory).
167	  - cycle-protocol.md `## ochid trailers`: path semantics,
168	    trailer-format example, `### Resolvability`,
169	    `### .vc-config.toml`.
170	  - cycle-protocol.md `### vc-x1 push wrapper`: push injects
171	    the `ochid:` trailers itself — don't hand-write them.

1	# Todo
2	
3	This file contains near term tasks with a short description
4	and reference links to more details.
5	
6	Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
7	line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
8	pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.
9	
10	## In Progress
11	
12	When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
13	problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
14	by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
15	   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
16	   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
17	   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
18	   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation
19	
20	**feat: bot-session --fields + --raw explorer**
21	
22	--raw reframed (2026-07-17 review): a cat-like verbatim dump
23	barely beats jq — the real want is *schema exploration*. The
24	parser keeps every field Anthropic writes while the typed layer
25	consumes a known subset; the difference is the unexplored
26	surface, so format changes surface themselves.
27	
28	- --fields: aggregated field inventory — dotted paths per entry
29	  type with count, value kinds, short samples
30	- --unknown: inventory minus the extractor's consumed set
31	  (KNOWN_PATHS, subtree semantics) — "what's new / unmodeled"
32	- --raw [--lines]: pretty-printed source lines for content
33	  drill-down; --lines unit is *source JSONL lines* here
34	- drift-over-time baseline across dated transcripts deferred to
35	  the discovery/index cycle
36	
37	- 0.70.2 feat: bot-session --fields + --raw explorer (done)
38	
39	## Todo
40	
41	 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
42	 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
43	 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run TODO.md` to renumber.
44	 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
45	 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
46	 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
47	 number. Long-tail entries
48	 live in [todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md). Use the
49	 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](/AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
50	 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
51	 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).
52	
53	1. **vc-x1 push: pause point between commit and publish
54	   stages.** The merge non-ff close-out is a three-step
55	   sequence:
56	   - commit the close-out pair locally (normal 1:1 commit
57	     stages, ochids injected both directions)
58	   - rebase the code-side close-out into the merge (chids
59	     survive rebase, so every ochid stays valid)
60	   - publish
61	
62	   Push has no supported stop after `commit-bot`, so today
63	   the recipe pre-commits both sides manually and resumes via
64	   `--from bookmark-set --yes` — skipping exactly the stages
65	   that inject `ochid:` trailers.
66	   - Add a stop after the commit stages (`--to commit-bot`
67	     or `--no-publish`; name open); the existing `--from
68	     bookmark-set` is already the resume half.
69	   - Retires the close-out workaround; the merge commit
70	     carries its code→bot ochid because it was injected
71	     normally, before the rebase.
72	   - Together with the Todo "push/sync: bookmark is
73	     code-repo-only; pin the bot repo to main", completes
74	     the trapezoidal-commit workflow (1:1 bot↔code
75	     throughout; the merge is a code-side-only shape
76	     operation).
77	   - Interim recipe only: the refactor program's
78	     [trapezoid close-out stage](notes/refactor-20260716.md#stage-trapezoid-close-out)
79	     is the end state; this pause point remains the manual
80	     path until it lands.
81	
82	2. **Refactor: typed jj facade → jj-lib in-process; end
83	   subprocess spawning.** Version-control operations are
84	   ~30 hand-rolled `run("jj", …)` spawns plus every
85	   mutation, with per-module private wrappers and raw-git
86	   vestiges in init — stderr parsing instead of typed
87	   errors, and jj's single-attempt index-lock acquisition
88	   (the push `bookmark-set` lock race in [bugs.md](notes/bugs.md))
89	   can't be retried where it fails. A multi-ladder program;
90	   the staged plan, design detail, and the eight absorbed
91	   former Todos live in
92	   [refactor-20260716.md](notes/refactor-20260716.md).
93	   - Stages in execution order: DRY facade → hygiene
94	     riders → facade owns topology → de-gitify init →
95	     split push.rs → jj-lib migration → push body-intro
96	     validation → trapezoid close-out → por → dual
97	     conversion.
98	3. **Version-number protocol is fragile — versions are
99	   baked into titles/bodies/todo/done/chores before the
100	   change lands.** The cycle protocol embeds an `X.Y.Z-N`
101	   version in commit titles and bodies, `## Todo` /
102	   `## Done` entries, and chores headers — all written
103	   while the work is in progress, i.e. before it lands.
104	   But version numbers are subject to change: in a public,
105	   merge-based flow (e.g. Linux), the version a change
106	   ships under is only fixed when it merges into `main`,
107	   so the landing version can't be anticipated while the
108	   work is underway. Pervasive version-in-text is
109	   therefore fragile for any non-linear / multi-contributor
110	   workflow. Promoted from Ideas at 0.65.2-0; slated for
111	   the cycle after 0.65.2.
112	   - Open question: what identifies a cycle's commits if
113	     not a pre-assigned version?
114	     - Needs to be unique within some agreed upon domain.
115	       A contributors email address would do it, but also
116	       a UUID (short-version) for a contribution. I could
117	       imagine a UUID generated from the initial email/issue
118	       that and then "version number" schema appended to that.
119	   - Surfaces to update once the identifier is chosen:
120	     cycle-protocol.md (title shape, Numbering), AGENTS.md
121	     (commit-recording headers), and the `vc-x1` validators
122	     that parse `(X.Y.Z)` strings.
123	4. **sync follow-up: extract `move-bookmark` command.** The
124	   "put the bookmark / `@` where it belongs" step at the end
125	   of sync (reposition logic) is useful standalone — e.g. the
126	   t1B scenario where `main` is right but `@` isn't on it —
127	   and deserves an honestly-named command instead of a mode.
128	   - `vc-x1 move-bookmark` (name open): no fetch; move `@`
129	     (and optionally the bookmark) onto a target under the
130	     same safety rules as sync's reposition step.
131	   - Sync's final step becomes a call to the same logic.
132	   - Follow-up to the 0.67.0 single-mode sync cycle.
133	5. **sync follow-up: push preflight in-process; drop
134	   `--check`; revisit push auto-rollback.** Push's preflight
135	   shells out to `vc-x1 sync --check` — a verify-only pass
136	   that is both racy (remote can move before the user's
137	   later apply) and not actually read-only (jj's fetch
138	   auto-ffs tracked bookmarks). Follow-up to the 0.67.0
139	   single-mode sync cycle.
140	   - Preflight becomes a real in-process sync (stop-on-error
141	     halts the push before anything is committed); the
142	     `sync --check` shell-out and PATH dependency go away.
143	   - Remove the deprecated hidden `--check` alias once
144	     nothing invokes it.
145	   - Apply the stop-on-error + `vc-x1 revert` philosophy to
146	     push's commit-stage rollback (today it auto-runs

148	6. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all

153	   for a pre-commit gate. Prereq for the pre-commit doc
154	   validators (Todo "pre-commit: single rule ...").
155	   - Rename the pair: `validate-todo` → `validate-numbering`,
156	     `fix-todo` → `fix-numbering` — they validate numbered-
157	     sequence integrity, not todos specifically.
158	   - Generic detection: for every `#…#` section, validate the
159	     column-0 `^\d+\.␠` entries form a contiguous 1..N run.
160	     Drops the Todo/Bugs special-casing; auto-covers

**Title:**
```
feat: bot-session --fields + --raw explorer
```

**Body:**
```
Work (0.70.2, single-commit cycle): --raw reframed at review
from a cat-like verbatim dump to schema exploration — the
parser keeps every field Anthropic writes while the typed
layer consumes a known subset; the difference is the
unexplored surface, so format changes surface themselves.
Also unifies --lines to source-JSONL-line units in every
view. Validated: fmt/clippy clean, all 6 suites green,
installed 0.70.2; all views exercised on real transcripts
(first --unknown run: 132 unmodeled paths in one 485-line
file).

- src/transcript.rs: exploration primitives
  - leaf_paths — dotted-path walker ([] for array elements,
    empty containers count as leaves)
  - KNOWN_PATHS + is_known — the extractor's consumed set
    with subtree semantics (message.content[].input covers
    everything beneath it)
  - parse_file removed — lost its last caller (each view
    reads the text itself for the source-line count)
- src/bot_session.rs: "Alternate views" flag group
  - --fields: per-entry-type inventory — path, value kinds,
    count, up to 3 short samples, "N paths across M entries"
    summary
  - --unknown: same view filtered to unmodeled paths
  - --raw: pretty-printed source lines; unparseable lines
    pass through verbatim; no summary or markers; conflicts
    with --fields
  - --per-line (implies --fields, composes with --unknown):
    one fields section per source line — "=== Index N:
    <type> [time] ===" + path/kind/value rows, malformed
    lines in place
  - --lines unified to source JSONL lines in every view
    (review-surfaced twice: --fields first ignored --lines;
    the conversation view sliced rendered lines, selecting
    different regions per item flags) — conversation view
    renders the entries in the slice with source-line
    elision markers and a "--lines selected K of L source
    lines" summary clause scoped to the slice; apply_lines
    and the rendered-slice summary removed
- src/bot_session/tests.rs: line_bounds cases pinned
  directly, render_source_slice added, summary format
  updated
- src/transcript/tests.rs: leaf_paths_walks,
  is_known_subtrees added; parse_file test removed
- tests/cli_bot_session.rs: cli_bot_session_alternate_views
  (fields known+unknown, sliced fields, raw pretty-print,
  truncated-line passthrough, --raw/--fields conflict,
  --per-line sections incl. malformed-in-place);
  cli_bot_session_lines rewritten for source units
- README.md: Alternate views subsection (four-row table,
  worked questions incl. a --per-line walk); --lines docs
  reworded to the single source-line unit; sliced-summary
  description updated
- src/main.rs: long_about gains the alternate views and the

- Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock: version 0.70.2
```

message.content:
What does this mean "every dotted path per entry type" in:
| `--fields` | Field inventory: every dotted path per entry type, with count, value kinds, and short samples |

content.text:
Two pieces of jargon packed together:

- **Dotted path** — the flattened address of a field inside the line's JSON tree: `message.content[].type` means "the `type` field of each element of the `content` array inside `message`". Nesting becomes dots, array elements become `[]`.
- **Per entry type** — the inventory is grouped by each line's top-level `"type"` value (`user`, `assistant`, `mode`, `file-history-snapshot`, …), because the same field name can mean different things on different line types. That's why the output has one `=== assistant (217 lines) ===` section, one `=== user … ===` section, and so on.

So the row means: "for each kind of line, list every field location observed, with how often it occurred, what value types it held, and a few example values."

That phrasing assumes the reader already knows both terms — let me say it plainer in the README:

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
s = open('README.md').read()
s = s.replace("""| `--fields` | Field inventory: every dotted path per entry type, with count, value kinds, and short samples |""",
"""| `--fields` | Field inventory, grouped by each line's `type`: every field observed (as a dotted path, e.g. `message.content[].type` — dots for nesting, `[]` for array elements) with its count, value kinds, and short samples |""")
open('README.md', 'w').write(s)
EOF
grep -n "Field inventory, grouped" README.md | head -1
